q: Which of the following BEST defines Software Engineering?
options: A systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software
The process of writing code to solve a problem
Designing user interfaces for software applications
Managing hardware resources for software execution
explanation: Software Engineering (IEEE definition) is the application of a systematic, disciplined, and quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software. It applies engineering principles to software creation â€” not just coding, but the full lifecycle including planning, design, testing, and maintenance.

q: The 'software crisis' of the late 1960s was primarily caused by:
options: Software projects consistently failing to deliver on time, within budget, or meeting requirements
explanation: The software crisis (late 1960sâ€“early 1970s) referred to the widespread failure of software projects â€” cost overruns, schedule delays, poor quality, and unmet user requirements. This crisis motivated the creation of software engineering as a discipline. The NATO Software Engineering Conference (1968) coined the term 'software engineering' as a response.

q: Which is the CORRECT order of phases in the classical Waterfall model?
options: Requirements â†’ Design â†’ Coding â†’ Testing â†’ Maintenance
Design â†’ Requirements â†’ Coding â†’ Testing â†’ Maintenance
Requirements â†’ Coding â†’ Design â†’ Testing â†’ Maintenance
Analysis â†’ Coding â†’ Design â†’ Testing â†’ Deployment
explanation: The Waterfall model follows a strict sequential order: Requirements â†’ System Design â†’ Implementation (Coding) â†’ Testing â†’ Deployment â†’ Maintenance. Each phase must be completed before the next begins, with no overlapping. This is the defining characteristic of the Waterfall model.

q: In the V-Model, the phase that corresponds to Unit Testing is:
explanation: The V-Model maps each development phase to a corresponding testing phase. Module/Detailed Design corresponds to Unit Testing. The mapping: Requirements â†” Acceptance Testing, System Design â†” System Testing, Integration Design â†” Integration Testing, Module Design â†” Unit Testing.

q: Which software development model is BEST suited when requirements are well-understood and unlikely to change?
explanation: The Waterfall model works best when requirements are clear, stable, and well-documented from the start. It is a linear sequential model with no provision for revisiting earlier phases. Agile and Iterative models handle changing requirements better. Spiral is suited for high-risk, large projects.

q: The Spiral model of software development was proposed by:
explanation: Barry Boehm proposed the Spiral model in 1986. It combines iterative development with the systematic aspects of the Waterfall model and emphasizes risk analysis at each spiral loop. Winston Royce is associated with the Waterfall model, Watts Humphrey with CMM/PSP, and Grady Booch with OOP and UML.

q: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of a good software process?
explanation: A good software process should be Understandable, Visible (progress can be measured), Supportable (tool support available), Acceptable, Reliable, Robust, Maintainable, and allow Rapid development. 'Ad-hoc and unplanned execution' is the hallmark of an immature, chaotic process â€” the opposite of good process.

q: In Agile methodology, a 'Sprint' typically lasts:
explanation: In Scrum (an Agile framework), a Sprint is a time-boxed iteration lasting 1 to 4 weeks (commonly 2 weeks). At the end of each Sprint, a potentially shippable product increment is delivered. This short cycle allows teams to adapt quickly to changing requirements.

q: Which phase of SDLC involves creating Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs) and Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams?
explanation: DFDs and ER diagrams are created during System Analysis and Design. DFDs model data flow through the system; ER diagrams model database structure. Requirement Gathering captures user needs in plain language. Coding implements the design. Testing verifies correctness.

q: A classic management software myth is:
options: Adding more programmers to a late project will speed it up
A general-purpose computer can run any software
All bugs can be fixed before release
explanation: Brooks' Law: 'Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later.' New developers need ramp-up time and increase communication overhead, slowing the project further. Fred Brooks documented this in 'The Mythical Man-Month' (1975). This is the most cited management myth in software engineering.

q: The primary difference between a software 'process' and a software 'product' is:
options: Process is the set of activities to build software; product is the resulting software artifact
Process is the final executable; product is the documentation
Process refers to hardware; product refers to software
Both terms mean the same thing in software engineering
explanation: A software Process is the set of activities, methods, practices, and transformations used to develop and maintain software. A software Product is the outcome â€” the actual software system, code, documentation, and related artifacts. The process determines the quality of the product.

q: Which SDLC phase produces the Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document?
explanation: The SRS (Software Requirements Specification) is the primary output of the Requirement Analysis phase. It formally describes functional requirements (what the software must do) and non-functional requirements (performance, security, etc.). It serves as a contract between customer and development team.

q: In the V-Model, System Testing corresponds to which development phase?
explanation: In the V-Model, the right (testing) side mirrors the left (development) side. System Testing verifies the entire integrated system against System Design specs. Mapping: System Design â†” System Testing. This ensures each design decision has a corresponding verification step.

q: Which SDLC model explicitly incorporates risk analysis in every phase?
explanation: The Spiral model uniquely includes a dedicated risk analysis step in every iteration. Each spiral loop has four quadrants: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, and Evaluation. This makes it ideal for large, high-risk projects where risks must be identified and mitigated continuously.

q: The Iterative model differs from the Waterfall model primarily because:
options: It allows revisiting and refining phases in repeated cycles
It is only used for small projects
explanation: The Iterative model builds software in repeated cycles, with each iteration producing a working version refined in subsequent cycles. Unlike Waterfall (strictly sequential), Iterative allows revisiting earlier phases. Each iteration passes through requirements, design, code, and test phases.

q: Which of the following is a customer myth about software development?
options: A vague statement of objectives is sufficient to begin writing programs
Once software is written, it is easy to change
Software projects always deliver on time
explanation: Customer myths include: (1) A vague objective is enough to start â€” false, requirements must be clear and detailed; (2) Software is easy to change once written â€” false, changes can be very costly. These myths lead to poorly specified projects and unrealistic expectations, contributing to project failure.

q: Which type of maintenance involves fixing bugs found after deployment?
explanation: Four types of software maintenance: (1) Corrective â€” fixing bugs/defects post-delivery; (2) Adaptive â€” modifying software for new environments (OS upgrade, new hardware); (3) Perfective â€” enhancing features or performance; (4) Preventive â€” restructuring code to prevent future problems. Bug fixing = Corrective Maintenance.

q: Which document is a core statement of Agile values?
explanation: The Agile Manifesto (2001), signed by 17 software practitioners, defines four core values: (1) Individuals and interactions over processes and tools; (2) Working software over comprehensive documentation; (3) Customer collaboration over contract negotiation; (4) Responding to change over following a plan.

q: In SDLC, the 'feasibility study' is conducted during which phase?
explanation: A feasibility study is conducted in the early Requirement Gathering/Planning phase to determine if the project is technically, financially, legally, and operationally viable before committing resources. Types include: technical, economic, legal, operational, and schedule feasibility.

q: Which of the following is NOT an Agile methodology?
explanation: Scrum, Kanban, and XP (Extreme Programming) are all Agile methodologies. PRINCE2 (Projects IN Controlled Environments) is a structured project management methodology â€” it is heavyweight, process-driven, and not Agile. It is used widely in government and large organizations in the UK and Europe.

q: The 'software crisis' led directly to the establishment of:
options: Software Engineering as a formal discipline
explanation: The software crisis (1960sâ€“70s) â€” widespread project failures, cost overruns, and unreliable software â€” directly motivated the establishment of Software Engineering as a formal discipline. The 1968 NATO conference is considered the birth of software engineering. The crisis showed that ad-hoc coding was insufficient for complex systems.

q: In the Waterfall model, what is the MAJOR disadvantage?
options: It is too expensive to implement
Customer feedback can only be incorporated at the very end
It does not include a testing phase
explanation: The biggest weakness of the Waterfall model is that the customer sees working software only at the very end of the project. If requirements were misunderstood, corrections are extremely costly. There is no mechanism for mid-project feedback. This 'Big Bang' delivery makes it risky for projects with unclear or evolving requirements.

q: Which SDLC phase converts design documents into executable code?
explanation: The Implementation (Coding) phase is where developers translate design specifications into source code using a programming language. Inputs are design documents (architecture diagrams, pseudocode, flowcharts); output is source code. This phase follows Design and precedes Testing.

q: In software design, 'modularity' means:
options: Dividing software into separate, independent, manageable components
Writing the entire program in a single file
explanation: Modularity divides a system into discrete, independent modules each handling specific functionality. Benefits: easier maintenance, better reusability, parallel development, and easier testing. High cohesion within modules (related functions together) and low coupling between modules (minimal dependencies) are desirable properties.

q: The Prototype model is BEST described as:
options: Building a final production-ready system in the first attempt
Building a working model to gather user feedback before building the final system
A model used exclusively for hardware development
Same as the Waterfall model but with an extra testing phase
explanation: The Prototype model builds a preliminary working model (prototype) to gather user feedback early in development. The prototype may be discarded (throwaway prototyping) or evolved into the final product (evolutionary prototyping). Especially useful when requirements are unclear; reduces risk of building the wrong product.

q: According to software engineering, which statement about software is TRUE?
options: Software does not deteriorate but it does become less useful over time due to changing requirements
Software quality decreases due to physical wear
Software and hardware have the same failure rate curves
explanation: Unlike hardware, software does not physically wear out. However, it deteriorates in a different sense: as requirements evolve, the original software becomes less suitable â€” a phenomenon called 'software aging' or 'technical debt.' Hardware has a bathtub failure curve; software's failure rate is high initially (bugs), drops, then slowly rises as changes introduce new defects.

q: The primary goal of the 'testing' phase in SDLC is:
options: To write test cases for documentation purposes only
To verify that the software meets specified requirements and find defects before deployment
To train end users on how to use the software
To optimize the performance of the database
explanation: The Testing phase aims to verify the software meets specified requirements (validation) and to detect and fix defects (verification) before deployment. Types include: Unit Testing, Integration Testing, System Testing, and User Acceptance Testing (UAT). Testing improves quality and reduces post-deployment defect costs.

q: In the Spiral model, the four quadrants in each cycle are:
explanation: Each loop of the Spiral model consists of four quadrants: (1) Planning â€” determine objectives and constraints; (2) Risk Analysis â€” identify and resolve risks; (3) Engineering â€” develop and test the product; (4) Evaluation â€” customer review and feedback. Risk Analysis is what distinguishes the Spiral model from all others.

q: Which of the following SDLC models is also called the 'Verification and Validation model'?
explanation: The V-Model is called the Verification and Validation model because it explicitly maps each development phase to a corresponding testing (verification/validation) phase. The left side of the 'V' represents development phases (verification); the right side represents testing phases (validation). It emphasizes quality from the start.

q: A practitioner myth in software engineering is:
options: Once we write the program, our job is done
Requirements always change during a project
Documentation is not needed for small projects
explanation: A common practitioner myth is that the job is done once code is written. In reality, maintenance accounts for 60â€“80% of total software cost and effort. Post-delivery activities (bug fixes, adaptations, enhancements) represent the largest portion of a software product's lifecycle cost.

q: Which SDLC model is characterized by delivering software in small, usable increments?
explanation: The Incremental Model divides the software into increments (pieces), each delivering a working subset of the final functionality. The first increment is often a core product; subsequent increments add features. This allows early delivery of partial functionality and reduces the all-or-nothing risk of Waterfall.

q: Software Engineering was born as a discipline primarily at which conference?
explanation: The NATO Software Engineering Conference held in Garmisch, Germany in 1968 is widely recognized as the birthplace of Software Engineering as a formal discipline. It was convened specifically to address the software crisis, and the term 'software engineering' was deliberately chosen to imply that software development needed the rigor of traditional engineering.

q: In Agile, the role responsible for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog is:
explanation: In Scrum, the Product Owner is responsible for maximizing the value of the product, managing and prioritizing the Product Backlog, and ensuring the team works on the highest-value items. The Scrum Master facilitates the process and removes impediments. The Development Team does the actual work.

q: Which of the following is a characteristic of software that distinguishes it from hardware?
options: Software is manufactured in a factory
Software is developed or engineered, not manufactured
explanation: Key characteristics of software: (1) Software is engineered/developed, not manufactured on assembly lines; (2) Software does not wear out physically; (3) Most software is custom-built rather than assembled from components; (4) Software is a logical rather than physical system element. These distinguish it fundamentally from hardware.

q: The term 'cohesion' in software design refers to:
options: The degree of interdependence between modules
How well the elements within a single module are related to each other
The number of functions in a program
The speed at which modules communicate
explanation: Cohesion measures how strongly related and focused the responsibilities of a single module are. High cohesion means a module does one specific, well-defined thing â€” this is desirable. Low cohesion means a module handles unrelated tasks â€” this is undesirable. In contrast, Coupling measures interdependence BETWEEN modules (low coupling is desired).

q: User Acceptance Testing (UAT) is performed by:
options: Developers to check their own code
QA engineers in a lab environment
End users to verify the software meets their business needs
explanation: UAT (User Acceptance Testing) is the final testing phase performed by actual end users or clients to verify the software meets their business requirements in a real or near-real environment. It is the last phase before production deployment. UAT confirms the software is 'fit for purpose' from the user's perspective.

q: The Waterfall model was first described by:
explanation: Winston Royce described what became known as the Waterfall model in his 1970 paper 'Managing the Development of Large Software Systems.' Ironically, Royce presented it as a flawed model and recommended iterative development. The name 'Waterfall' comes from the visual appearance of phases cascading downward like a waterfall.

q: Which of the following best describes 'Adaptive Maintenance'?
options: Fixing a bug that causes incorrect calculation
Modifying software to work with a new operating system or hardware
Adding a new feature requested by the user
explanation: Adaptive Maintenance modifies software to keep it functional in a changing environment â€” new OS, new hardware, new regulations, or integration with new third-party systems. It is NOT about fixing bugs (that's Corrective) or adding features (that's Perfective). Example: updating an app to run on a new version of Windows.

q: In which SDLC model is the entire system built in a single pass with no iteration?
explanation: The classic Waterfall model builds the entire system in a single linear pass through all phases with no iteration back to earlier phases. This 'build it once' approach is its defining characteristic and biggest limitation. All other listed models (Agile, Incremental, Spiral) involve multiple iterations or cycles.

q: The main purpose of a 'Design Review' in SDLC is:
options: To evaluate design documents for correctness, completeness, and adherence to requirements
explanation: A Design Review is a formal inspection of design documents to verify they correctly and completely represent the requirements, are internally consistent, and are feasible to implement. Early defect detection through reviews is far cheaper than fixing defects found during testing or after deployment.

q: Which SDLC phase is most concerned with 'what' the system should do (not 'how')?
explanation: Requirements Analysis defines WHAT the system should do â€” the functional and non-functional requirements from the user's perspective â€” without specifying HOW it will be implemented. The Design phase defines HOW the system will be implemented (architecture, data structures, algorithms). This what-vs-how distinction is fundamental.

q: Extreme Programming (XP) is associated with which of the following practices?
explanation: XP (Extreme Programming), an Agile methodology created by Kent Beck, emphasizes engineering practices: Test-Driven Development (write tests before code), Pair Programming (two developers at one workstation), Continuous Integration, Refactoring, and Simple Design. It is the opposite of documentation-heavy, phase-gate approaches.

q: Which of the following statements about software maintenance is CORRECT?
options: Maintenance typically consumes less than 20% of total software cost
Maintenance is the cheapest phase of the SDLC
Maintenance can consume 60â€“80% of total software lifecycle cost
Maintenance is only needed for legacy systems
explanation: Studies show software maintenance accounts for 60â€“80% of the total software lifecycle cost â€” making it the most expensive phase overall. This is why maintainability is a critical quality attribute. Corrective, adaptive, and perfective maintenance activities continue for the entire operational life of the software.

q: The 'coupling' between two modules should be:
options: As high as possible for better integration
As low as possible to minimize dependencies
Equal to the cohesion of each module
explanation: Coupling measures the degree of interdependence between modules. Low coupling is desirable because: changes to one module minimally affect others, modules are easier to understand independently, and reuse is easier. High cohesion + Low coupling is the golden rule of good modular design.

q: In Agile Scrum, the 'Daily Scrum' (Stand-up) meeting is limited to:
explanation: The Daily Scrum (Stand-up) is time-boxed to 15 minutes. Each team member answers three questions: (1) What did I do yesterday? (2) What will I do today? (3) Are there any impediments? The brevity keeps it focused and prevents it from becoming a status meeting. The Scrum Master ensures the time-box is respected.

q: Which of the following is a non-functional requirement?
options: The system shall allow users to log in with a username and password
The system shall process 10,000 transactions per second
The system shall generate monthly reports
The system shall send email notifications
explanation: Non-functional requirements (NFRs) define system qualities â€” how well the system performs â€” rather than what it does. Performance (10,000 TPS), security, reliability, scalability, and usability are NFRs. The other options (login, reports, notifications) describe specific behaviors â€” these are functional requirements.

q: The concept of 'software reuse' became a major theme in software engineering because:
options: It reduces development time, cost, and increases reliability by using proven components
It eliminates the need for testing
explanation: Software reuse â€” using existing components, libraries, or frameworks rather than building from scratch â€” reduces development time and cost, improves reliability (proven components have fewer defects), and improves consistency. It is a cornerstone of component-based development and object-oriented programming. It does NOT eliminate testing.

q: Which of the following accurately describes the relationship between Verification and Validation?
options: Verification: Are we building the product right? Validation: Are we building the right product?
Verification: Are we building the right product? Validation: Are we building the product right?
Both terms mean the same thing
Verification is done by users; Validation is done by developers
explanation: Classic distinction by Barry Boehm: Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' â€” checking that the software conforms to its specification (internal consistency, correctness). Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' â€” checking that the software meets actual user needs. Both are essential to quality assurance.

q: In SDLC, 'black-box testing' means:
options: Testing done by developers who know the internal code
Testing the software based on its external behavior without knowledge of internal implementation
explanation: Black-box testing treats the software as a black box â€” testers evaluate behavior based on inputs and outputs without any knowledge of the internal code/logic. It tests functional requirements. Contrast with White-box testing (glass-box), where testers have full knowledge of the internal structure and test code paths directly.

q: The SDLC phase that involves identifying system entities, their attributes, and relationships is part of:
explanation: Identifying system entities, their attributes, and relationships is a key activity in System Analysis and Logical Design. This produces Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams and logical data models. Physical Design then translates these into actual database schemas, file structures, and implementation-specific designs.

q: Which of the following correctly lists the phases of the Waterfall model in order?
options: Requirements â†’ Design â†’ Implementation â†’ Testing â†’ Deployment â†’ Maintenance
Design â†’ Requirements â†’ Testing â†’ Implementation â†’ Maintenance
Requirements â†’ Implementation â†’ Design â†’ Testing â†’ Deployment
Planning â†’ Coding â†’ Testing â†’ Design â†’ Deployment
explanation: The Waterfall model follows a strict sequential order: Requirements â†’ Design â†’ Implementation â†’ Testing â†’ Deployment â†’ Maintenance. Each phase must be completed before the next begins.

q: The Waterfall model is BEST suited for which type of project?
options: Projects where requirements are well-understood and unlikely to change
Projects requiring rapid delivery of working software
explanation: The Waterfall model works best when requirements are clear, stable, and well-documented upfront. It is not suitable for projects where requirements evolve during development.

q: Which is a major disadvantage of the Waterfall model?
options: It allows too much customer involvement
Working software is delivered very early
Customer cannot see the product until late in the development cycle
explanation: In the Waterfall model, working software is only produced at the end of the entire lifecycle. Customers cannot review the product until the final stages, making it risky if requirements were misunderstood.

q: Which phase of the Waterfall model involves converting the design into executable code?
explanation: The Implementation phase (also called Coding or Programming phase) is where the actual source code is written based on the design documents produced in the earlier phase.

q: In the Waterfall model, the System Design phase produces which primary output?
explanation: The System Design phase takes the requirements specification and produces architecture diagrams, data flow diagrams, and detailed design documents that guide the implementation phase.

q: Which advantage of the Waterfall model is most significant?
options: Simple, easy to understand and use with clear milestones
explanation: The Waterfall model's primary advantage is its simplicity and clarity. Each phase has clear deliverables and milestones, making it easy to manage and understand. This structured approach works well for teams new to software development.

q: Going back to a previous phase in the Waterfall model is considered:
options: Expensive and difficult, making the model inflexible
Easy because the model is designed for iteration
explanation: The Waterfall model is sequential and non-iterative. Going back to fix issues in a previous phase is very costly because each phase builds on the previous one. This inflexibility is one of its major drawbacks.

q: The Waterfall model is NOT recommended when:
options: The project is large and complex with a fixed budget
Requirements are clear and will not change
Requirements are unclear and expected to change frequently
Documentation is required at each phase
explanation: The Waterfall model is unsuitable for projects where requirements are unclear or likely to change, because it requires complete requirements before design begins and does not support revisiting phases easily.

q: In the V-Model, what does the left side of the 'V' represent?
explanation: In the V-Model, the left side of the V represents the development/verification phases (Requirements â†’ System Design â†’ Architecture Design â†’ Module Design â†’ Coding). Each left-side phase has a corresponding testing phase on the right.

q: In the V-Model, Unit Testing corresponds to which development phase?
explanation: In the V-Model, Unit Testing is the counterpart of Low-Level Design (Module/Detailed Design). Unit tests verify that individual modules work correctly as designed at the module level.

q: In the V-Model, Acceptance Testing corresponds to which development phase?
explanation: Acceptance Testing corresponds to Requirements Analysis in the V-Model. Acceptance tests validate that the final system meets the user requirements defined at the start of the project.

q: What is the key difference between Verification and Validation in the V-Model?
options: Verification checks if we built the right product; Validation checks if we built the product right
Verification checks if we built the product right; Validation checks if we built the right product
Verification is done after deployment; Validation is done during coding
There is no difference; both terms are interchangeable
explanation: Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' (process-oriented, checks against specifications). Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' (product-oriented, checks against user needs). This is a classic definition attributed to Barry Boehm.

q: In the V-Model, Integration Testing corresponds to which development phase?
explanation: Integration Testing corresponds to High-Level Design (Architecture/System Design) in the V-Model. It verifies that different modules interact correctly as described in the architectural design.

q: Which model is an extension of the Waterfall model that emphasizes testing at every development stage?
explanation: The V-Model (Verification and Validation Model) is an extension of the Waterfall model. Instead of being linear, it bends upward after coding to show that each development phase has a corresponding testing phase, emphasizing testing throughout.

q: System Testing in the V-Model corresponds to which left-side phase?
explanation: System Testing corresponds to System Design in the V-Model. It verifies the complete integrated system against the system design specifications to ensure all components work together as designed.

q: In Throwaway Prototyping, the prototype is:
options: Refined and evolved into the final product
Discarded after requirements are understood, and the actual system is built from scratch
Used as the final deliverable to the customer
explanation: In Throwaway (Rapid/Close-ended) Prototyping, the prototype is built quickly to understand requirements, then discarded. The final system is built from scratch using the knowledge gained. The prototype serves only as a requirements elicitation tool.

q: In Evolutionary Prototyping, the prototype is:
options: Built only for user interface mockups
Continuously refined and evolved into the final product
explanation: In Evolutionary Prototyping (also called Breadboard Prototyping), the prototype is not discarded. Instead, it is iteratively refined based on user feedback until it becomes the final working system.

q: The primary purpose of a software prototype is to:
options: Replace the need for requirements documentation
Help understand and clarify user requirements early in the project
explanation: The primary purpose of prototyping is to quickly build a working model of the software to help stakeholders visualize and clarify requirements early, reducing the risk of building the wrong product.

q: Which prototyping approach is more suitable when requirements are unclear and experimentation is needed?
explanation: Throwaway Prototyping is ideal when requirements are very unclear and the team needs to experiment. Since the prototype is discarded, poor design choices don't affect the final product's quality or maintainability.

q: A disadvantage of Evolutionary Prototyping is:
options: It wastes time building throwaway code
Poor documentation and potentially poor system structure due to continuous changes
explanation: A key risk in Evolutionary Prototyping is that as the prototype evolves, documentation may lag behind and the system structure may degrade due to frequent patches and changes. This can lead to a poorly structured final product.

q: In the Incremental model, software is:
options: Built entirely and delivered at once at the end
Built in small, functional pieces (increments) delivered over time
Developed only after all risks are analyzed
explanation: The Incremental model divides the system into small, manageable increments. Each increment adds functional capability and is developed through all lifecycle phases. Working software is delivered after each increment.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of the Incremental model?
options: The complete system must be defined before development starts
Working software is delivered early and users can provide feedback sooner
It requires no integration testing between increments
It eliminates the need for design documentation
explanation: A major advantage of the Incremental model is that working software is delivered after each increment, allowing users to provide early feedback and get value sooner. High-priority features can be delivered in the first increments.

q: The Incremental model is different from the Iterative model in that:
options: Incremental adds new functionality each cycle; Iterative refines the whole system each cycle
Incremental refines the whole system; Iterative adds new functionality
Both models are identical in approach
Incremental is only used for small projects
explanation: In the Incremental model, each iteration adds NEW functionality (a new increment/slice of the system). In the Iterative model, the complete system is built in rough form first and then refined in each iteration.

q: Which model is most appropriate when core functionality must be delivered early but additional features can come later?
explanation: The Incremental model is ideal when core, high-priority features need to be deployed quickly. The system is built in increments, with the most critical features delivered first, and additional features added in later increments.

q: The Spiral model was proposed by:
explanation: The Spiral model was proposed by Barry Boehm in 1986. It combines elements of both design and prototyping-in-stages, and is notable for its emphasis on risk analysis in each iteration (spiral).

q: The four quadrants of the Spiral model are:
options: Plan â†’ Design â†’ Build â†’ Test
Determine Objectives â†’ Identify and Resolve Risks â†’ Development and Testing â†’ Plan Next Iteration
Requirements â†’ Coding â†’ Integration â†’ Deployment
Inception â†’ Elaboration â†’ Construction â†’ Transition
explanation: Each loop of the Spiral model has four quadrants: (1) Determine objectives, alternatives, and constraints; (2) Evaluate alternatives, identify and resolve risks; (3) Develop and verify next-level product; (4) Plan the next iteration.

q: The most distinctive feature of the Spiral model compared to other SDLC models is:
options: It follows a strict sequential approach
It incorporates risk analysis at every iteration
It delivers software in a single release
It does not involve customer feedback
explanation: The defining characteristic of the Spiral model is its risk-driven approach. At every spiral loop, risks are explicitly identified, analyzed, and resolved before proceeding. This makes it suitable for large, complex, high-risk projects.

q: The Spiral model is MOST appropriate for:
options: Large, complex projects where risk management is critical
Projects with a fixed budget and no uncertainty
explanation: The Spiral model is designed for large, high-risk projects where requirements may evolve and risks must be actively managed. Its iterative nature and built-in risk analysis make it ideal for mission-critical systems.

q: In the Spiral model, a 'spiral' or loop represents:
options: A single phase of the Waterfall model
One complete pass through all four quadrants, delivering an increment of the product
explanation: Each loop (spiral) in the Spiral model represents one complete iteration through all four quadrants: planning, risk analysis, engineering, and evaluation. Each spiral results in a more refined version of the software product.

q: Which is a disadvantage of the Spiral model?
options: It does not support risk management
It is too rigid and does not allow changes
It is expensive and complex; requires expertise in risk assessment
It cannot be used for large projects
explanation: The Spiral model's major disadvantages include high cost, complexity, and the need for expert risk assessors. It can be difficult to manage and may not be suitable for smaller projects where such overhead is not justified.

q: The RAD (Rapid Application Development) model emphasizes:
options: Very short development cycles using component reuse and code generation tools
Strict sequential phases with no user involvement
explanation: RAD focuses on extremely rapid development (typically 60â€“90 days) using techniques like component reuse, CASE tools, code generators, and joint application development (JAD) workshops to speed up the development process.

q: The typical phases of the RAD model are:
options: Plan â†’ Risk â†’ Develop â†’ Test
Requirements Planning â†’ User Design â†’ Construction â†’ Cutover
Inception â†’ Elaboration â†’ Construction â†’ Transition
Analysis â†’ Design â†’ Code â†’ Test â†’ Deploy
explanation: The RAD model has four phases: (1) Requirements Planning â€“ combining elements of system planning and analysis; (2) User Design â€“ extensive user interaction and prototyping; (3) Construction â€“ rapid coding; (4) Cutover â€“ final testing, user training, and deployment.

q: RAD model is NOT suitable when:
options: High-quality components are available for reuse
Developer and customer are committed to rapid activities
Technical risk is high and the system cannot be modularized
The system can be properly modularized
explanation: RAD is not suitable when technical risk is high (e.g., new technology) or the system cannot be divided into modules. It also fails when developer/user commitment is lacking or when high-performance tuning is required.

q: The RAD model requires:
options: Strong commitment from both developers and users throughout the process
Complete requirements specification before development begins
Extensive risk analysis before each iteration
explanation: RAD depends heavily on strong, committed involvement from both developers and users, especially during the User Design phase. Without this commitment, the rapid iterations and prototyping that RAD relies on cannot be effective.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of the RAD model?
options: Suitable for projects with high technical risk
Reduced development time due to component reuse and rapid prototyping
Works best for large teams with many developers
explanation: RAD's main advantage is dramatically reduced development time through component reuse, parallel development by multiple teams, use of CASE tools, and heavy use of prototyping. This leads to faster delivery and early user feedback.

q: The Unified Process (RUP) was developed by:
explanation: The Rational Unified Process (RUP) was developed by Rational Software (now part of IBM) by Ivar Jacobson, Grady Booch, and James Rumbaugh â€” the 'Three Amigos' who also created UML.

q: The four phases of RUP (Rational Unified Process) are:
options: Plan â†’ Design â†’ Build â†’ Test
Inception â†’ Elaboration â†’ Construction â†’ Transition
Requirements â†’ Analysis â†’ Design â†’ Implementation
Analysis â†’ Design â†’ Coding â†’ Testing
explanation: RUP has four phases: Inception (scope and feasibility), Elaboration (architecture baseline and risk mitigation), Construction (building the product), and Transition (deployment and user adoption). Each phase can have multiple iterations.

q: The primary goal of the Inception phase in RUP is:
options: To write all the source code
To establish the business case and scope of the project
To refine the architecture and mitigate high risks
To deploy the software to end users
explanation: In RUP's Inception phase, the goal is to establish the project's scope, feasibility, and business case. Key activities include identifying major use cases, estimating costs/schedule, and deciding whether to proceed with the project.

q: In RUP, the Elaboration phase primarily focuses on:
options: Initial business case and stakeholder identification
Establishing an architectural baseline and resolving high-risk elements
Building most of the system functionality
Deploying the product and training users
explanation: The Elaboration phase in RUP focuses on creating a stable architectural baseline, addressing the most significant risks, and ensuring the project plan is accurate. The architecture is proven through prototypes or architectural spikes.

q: Which RUP phase involves building the complete system through multiple iterations?
explanation: The Construction phase in RUP is where most of the development happens. All remaining use cases are implemented, tested, and integrated. The product grows from an architectural baseline to a complete system ready for release.

q: The Transition phase in RUP includes:
options: Defining the project scope and business case
Beta testing, user training, and deployment to the production environment
Writing unit tests for all modules
explanation: The Transition phase covers everything needed to get the software into the hands of end users: beta testing, performance tuning, user training, documentation, and deployment. The goal is a smooth transition to the production environment.

q: RUP is described as 'use-case driven' because:
options: It uses the Waterfall model for planning
Use cases are the primary artifact for capturing requirements and driving design and testing
It only applies to web-based applications
Use cases replace all other forms of documentation
explanation: RUP is use-case driven, meaning use cases are the central artifact. They define the system's functional requirements, drive the design and implementation, and form the basis for test cases, ensuring all development aligns with user needs.

q: Which statement about the Waterfall model is TRUE?
options: Testing is done only after all coding is complete
Each phase can overlap with the next
explanation: In the classic Waterfall model, testing begins only after the implementation phase is fully complete. This means defects discovered during testing are expensive to fix as they may require changes to design or requirements.

q: The V-Model gets its name because:
options: When drawn, the development phases on the left and testing phases on the right form a V shape
explanation: The model is called V-Model because its diagram looks like the letter V: development phases descend on the left side, coding is at the bottom vertex, and testing phases ascend on the right side, with corresponding phases connected horizontally.

q: Which model combines the systematic nature of the Waterfall model with the iterative philosophy of prototyping?
explanation: The Spiral model combines the systematic, controlled nature of the Waterfall model with the iterative, repeated nature of prototyping. It adds risk analysis to each iteration, making it a risk-driven iterative model.

q: In which SDLC model is the concept of 'risk-driven' most central?
explanation: The Spiral model is fundamentally risk-driven. One of its four quadrants is entirely dedicated to risk identification, analysis, and resolution. The entire model is driven by the principle of managing and reducing project risks at each iteration.

q: Which SDLC model is BEST suited for a government defense project with high technical complexity and unknown risks?
explanation: Government defense projects are large, complex, and high-risk. The Spiral model is ideal because it explicitly addresses risk management at every iteration, accommodates changing requirements, and allows incremental delivery â€” all critical for such projects.

q: A software company needs to deliver a business application within 60 days using reusable components. Which model is MOST appropriate?
explanation: RAD (Rapid Application Development) is designed for fast delivery, typically within 60â€“90 days. It heavily uses reusable components, parallel development, and prototyping tools to compress the development timeline.

q: Which of the following best describes an 'increment' in the Incremental model?
options: A complete but partial version of the system with a defined subset of features
A throwaway prototype for requirements gathering
explanation: An increment is a complete, deliverable slice of the software system. It includes a defined subset of features and is fully designed, coded, and tested. Each increment builds on the previous ones until the entire system is complete.

q: The Waterfall model's 'Maintenance' phase involves:
options: Correcting faults, improving performance, and adapting the system after deployment
explanation: The Maintenance phase is the final phase of the Waterfall model, occurring after deployment. It involves corrective maintenance (fixing bugs), adaptive maintenance (adapting to new environments), and perfective maintenance (improving performance or features).

q: Which model is characterized by the phrase 'build a little, test a little, deploy a little'?
explanation: The Incremental model is described as 'build a little, test a little, deploy a little.' Each increment goes through design, development, and testing before delivery, allowing partial deployment of functionality progressively.

q: RUP is described as 'architecture-centric' because:
options: It focuses on database architecture only
The software architecture serves as the primary artifact guiding all development activities
It uses architectural patterns to replace use cases
Architecture is only defined in the Transition phase
explanation: RUP is architecture-centric because the software architecture is the central artifact around which the project is organized. A stable architecture baseline, established in the Elaboration phase, guides all subsequent development, testing, and deployment.

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the four values stated in the Agile Manifesto?
options: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools
Responding to change over following a plan
Delivering on time over delivering value
explanation: The four Agile Manifesto values are: (1) Individuals and interactions over processes and tools, (2) Working software over comprehensive documentation, (3) Customer collaboration over contract negotiation, (4) Responding to change over following a plan. 'Delivering on time over delivering value' is not one of them.

q: The Agile Manifesto was signed in which year?
explanation: The Agile Manifesto was created and signed in February 2001 by 17 software developers at a ski resort in Snowbird, Utah.

q: How many principles are listed under the Agile Manifesto?
explanation: The Agile Manifesto has 4 core values and 12 supporting principles that guide agile software development.

q: According to the Agile Manifesto, which is valued MORE?
options: Processes and tools over individuals and interactions
explanation: One of the four Agile Manifesto values explicitly states: 'Customer collaboration over contract negotiation', meaning customer collaboration is valued more.

q: Which Agile Manifesto principle states the highest priority?
options: Satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software
Welcome changing requirements, even late in development
Business people and developers must work together daily
explanation: The first and highest priority principle of the Agile Manifesto is: 'Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.'

q: In Scrum, who is responsible for maximizing the value of the product?
explanation: The Product Owner is responsible for maximizing the value of the product and managing the Product Backlog. They represent the stakeholders and the business.

q: What is the recommended size of a Scrum Development Team?
explanation: The Scrum Guide recommends a Development Team of 3 to 9 members. Fewer than 3 lacks sufficient skills, and more than 9 requires too much coordination.

q: Which Scrum artifact represents the ordered list of everything needed in the product?
explanation: The Product Backlog is the ordered list of everything that is known to be needed in the product. It is the single source of requirements and is managed by the Product Owner.

q: What is the maximum recommended duration of a Sprint in Scrum?
explanation: According to the Scrum Guide, a Sprint is time-boxed to one month or less. Most teams use 2-week sprints, but the maximum is one month.

q: Which Scrum ceremony is used to inspect the Sprint Backlog and adapt the plan for the next 24 hours?
explanation: The Daily Scrum (also called Daily Standup) is a 15-minute time-boxed event for the Development Team to synchronize activities and create a plan for the next 24 hours.

q: During Sprint Planning, what is the maximum time-box for a one-month Sprint?
explanation: Sprint Planning is time-boxed to a maximum of 8 hours for a one-month Sprint. For shorter Sprints, the event is usually shorter proportionally.

q: What is the output of a Sprint Review?
explanation: The Sprint Review results in a revised Product Backlog that defines the probable Product Backlog items for the next Sprint. Stakeholders collaborate on what to do next.

q: Who facilitates the Sprint Retrospective in Scrum?
explanation: The Scrum Master facilitates the Sprint Retrospective. The Scrum Master ensures the event takes place and that all participants understand its purpose.

q: What does the Sprint Backlog contain?
options: All features planned for the entire product
Sprint Goal, selected Product Backlog items, and a plan for delivering the Increment
Only the user stories for the current sprint
The Definition of Done for each story
explanation: The Sprint Backlog consists of the Sprint Goal (why), the set of Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint (what), and an actionable plan for delivering the Increment (how).

q: In Scrum, what is 'velocity'?
options: The speed at which the Scrum Master resolves impediments
The number of story points completed by the team per sprint
The rate of defect discovery during testing
The time taken to complete the Daily Scrum
explanation: Velocity is the amount of work (measured in story points or similar units) a Scrum team completes during a Sprint. It is used for forecasting and planning future Sprints.

q: A burndown chart in Scrum tracks which of the following?
options: The number of bugs found per day
The remaining work in the Sprint over time
The team members' daily hours worked
The number of features completed overall
explanation: A Sprint Burndown Chart shows the remaining work (story points or tasks) in the Sprint over time. The ideal line goes from total story points on day 1 to zero on the last day.

q: Which role in Scrum is responsible for removing impediments that block the Development Team?
explanation: The Scrum Master is a servant-leader who helps the Development Team by removing impediments, protecting the team from outside interruptions, and facilitating Scrum events.

q: In Kanban, what does WIP stand for?
explanation: WIP stands for Work In Progress. Kanban limits WIP to prevent bottlenecks, reduce context switching, and improve flow. Each column on a Kanban board has a WIP limit.

q: Which of the following is a core principle of Kanban?
options: Assign story points to each task
explanation: Visualizing the workflow is a core Kanban principle. A Kanban board makes work visible, allowing team members to see the state of every task and identify bottlenecks.

q: What metric in Kanban measures the time from when work starts to when it is completed?
explanation: Cycle Time measures the time from when work actually starts (moved to 'In Progress') to when it is done. Lead Time measures from request creation to completion (includes wait time).

q: Which Extreme Programming (XP) practice involves two programmers working at a single workstation?
explanation: Pair Programming is an XP practice where two programmers work together at one workstation. One writes code (driver) while the other reviews (navigator), improving quality and knowledge sharing.

q: In Test-Driven Development (TDD), what is the correct order of steps?
options: Write code â†’ Write test â†’ Refactor
Write test â†’ Write code â†’ Refactor
Refactor â†’ Write test â†’ Write code
Write code â†’ Refactor â†’ Write test
explanation: TDD follows the Red-Green-Refactor cycle: (1) Write a failing test (Red), (2) Write the minimum code to pass the test (Green), (3) Refactor the code while keeping tests passing.

q: BDD (Behavior-Driven Development) uses which format for writing scenarios?
explanation: BDD uses the Given-When-Then format: Given (precondition/context), When (action taken), Then (expected outcome). Tools like Cucumber and SpecFlow use this format (Gherkin syntax).

q: Which XP practice ensures that all code is integrated and tested frequently, often multiple times a day?
explanation: Continuous Integration (CI) is an XP practice where developers integrate their code into a shared repository frequently (multiple times per day). Each integration is verified by automated build and tests.

q: What is a 'user story' in Agile development?
options: A detailed technical specification of a feature
A short description of a feature from the end user's perspective
A bug report filed by the customer
A test case for a specific functionality
explanation: A user story is a short, simple description of a feature told from the perspective of the person who desires the new capability. Format: 'As a [user], I want [goal] so that [benefit].'

q: The INVEST criteria for user stories stands for which of the following?
options: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable
Integrated, Normalized, Verified, Executable, Structured, Traceable
Incremental, Needed, Validated, Efficient, Scalable, Tested
Independent, Necessary, Verified, Expandable, Sized, Timely
explanation: INVEST is a mnemonic for good user story criteria: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small (or Sized appropriately), and Testable. It was created by Bill Wake.

q: In Agile, what is the 'Definition of Done' (DoD)?
options: The sprint goal set by the Product Owner
A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete
The acceptance criteria for a single user story
The release criteria approved by the customer
explanation: The Definition of Done (DoD) is a shared understanding within the Scrum Team of what it means for work to be complete. It ensures transparency and guides the Development Team in knowing when a Product Backlog item is done.

q: Which of the following best describes 'Lean Software Development'?
options: A methodology focusing on large upfront planning
Applying lean manufacturing principles to software development to eliminate waste
A framework that uses only pair programming and TDD
A scaled agile framework for enterprises
explanation: Lean Software Development, adapted by Mary and Tom Poppendieck from lean manufacturing (Toyota Production System), focuses on eliminating waste, amplifying learning, deciding late, delivering fast, empowering the team, building integrity in, and seeing the whole.

q: Which of the following is identified as 'waste' in Lean Software Development?
explanation: Lean Software Development identifies 7 types of waste (muda): Partially done work, Extra processes, Extra features, Task switching, Waiting, Motion, and Defects. Partially done work is a classic waste.

q: SAFe stands for which of the following?
explanation: SAFe stands for Scaled Agile Framework. It is a set of organization and workflow patterns for implementing agile practices at enterprise scale, combining Scrum, Kanban, XP, and Lean.

q: In SAFe, what is the term for a group of Agile teams that work together on a larger solution?
explanation: An Agile Release Train (ART) is a long-lived team of Agile teams (typically 50-125 people) in SAFe that work together to deliver value. The ART is the primary delivery vehicle in SAFe.

q: In SAFe, a Program Increment (PI) typically lasts how long?
explanation: A Program Increment (PI) in SAFe is a time-box of typically 8-12 weeks during which an Agile Release Train delivers incremental value. A PI consists of several Sprints/Iterations plus an Innovation and Planning (IP) iteration.

q: Which of the following is a key difference between Agile and Waterfall?
options: Waterfall allows iterative development; Agile does not
Agile embraces change throughout development; Waterfall resists change after requirements are fixed
Waterfall delivers working software every sprint
Agile requires complete documentation before coding begins
explanation: A key difference is that Agile embraces and adapts to changing requirements throughout the project, while Waterfall treats change as an exception after the requirements phase, making it costly to change course later.

q: In Waterfall model, which phase comes immediately after the Requirements phase?
explanation: In the Waterfall model, the phases are sequential: Requirements â†’ System Design â†’ Implementation (Coding) â†’ Integration and Testing â†’ Deployment â†’ Maintenance. System Design follows Requirements.

q: Which of the following is true about the Agile approach to documentation?
options: Agile requires no documentation at all
Agile values comprehensive documentation over working software
Agile values working software over comprehensive documentation but does not eliminate documentation
Agile produces more documentation than Waterfall
explanation: The Agile Manifesto values 'Working software over comprehensive documentation' â€” this does not mean no documentation, but that working software is the primary measure of progress. Necessary documentation is still produced.

q: A Sprint Retrospective focuses on which of the following?
options: Reviewing the product increment with stakeholders
Inspecting the team's process and identifying improvements
explanation: The Sprint Retrospective is an opportunity for the Scrum Team to inspect itself and create a plan for improvements to be enacted during the next Sprint. It focuses on people, relationships, process, and tools.

q: Which Agile framework is most associated with limiting Work in Progress (WIP) to improve flow?
explanation: Kanban is the Agile framework most associated with WIP limits. Limiting WIP reduces multitasking, exposes bottlenecks, and improves the flow of work through the system.

q: In Extreme Programming (XP), what is the practice of restructuring existing code without changing its external behavior called?
explanation: Refactoring is the XP practice of restructuring existing computer code without changing its external behavior. It improves code quality, readability, and maintainability.

q: Which Agile principle addresses sustainable development?
options: Agile processes promote sustainable development; the sponsors, developers, and users should be able to maintain a constant pace indefinitely
The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from self-organizing teams
Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances agility
explanation: One of the 12 Agile principles states: 'Agile processes promote sustainable development. The sponsors, developers, and users should be able to maintain a constant pace indefinitely.' This prevents burnout and ensures long-term productivity.

q: What is a 'Product Backlog Refinement' (grooming) in Scrum?
options: The process of deleting old items from the backlog
The activity of adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items
The Sprint Review meeting with stakeholders
The final acceptance testing of the product
explanation: Product Backlog Refinement (formerly called grooming) is the ongoing activity in which the Product Owner and the Development Team add detail, estimates, and order to items in the Product Backlog. It typically consumes no more than 10% of the team's capacity.

q: In Scrum, who can cancel a Sprint?
explanation: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint. A Sprint can be cancelled if the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, though this is rare and can be traumatic for the team.

q: Which of the following best describes a 'spike' in Agile?
options: A time-boxed research or investigation task to reduce uncertainty
A critical bug that must be fixed immediately
An unplanned item added to the sprint
explanation: A spike is a time-boxed research activity (from XP) used to explore a solution, investigate a technology, or reduce uncertainty around a complex user story. The outcome is knowledge, not working software.

q: In Agile, story points are primarily used for:
options: Measuring time spent on each story
Estimating the relative effort/complexity of user stories
Counting the number of lines of code written
Tracking the number of defects found
explanation: Story points are a unit of measure used in Agile to estimate the relative effort, complexity, and risk of user stories. They are relative, not absolute, and help teams compare stories to each other.

q: The Fibonacci sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21...) is commonly used in Agile for:
explanation: The Fibonacci sequence is commonly used in Planning Poker (a story point estimation technique) because the increasing gaps reflect the inherent uncertainty in estimating larger items. Teams use cards with Fibonacci numbers to estimate.

q: Which Lean Software Development principle states that decisions should be postponed until they can be made with the most information?
explanation: 'Decide as Late as Possible' (also called Defer Commitment) is one of the 7 Lean principles. Delaying irreversible decisions until the last responsible moment preserves flexibility and allows decisions to be made with more information.

q: In XP, 'the customer is always available' refers to which practice?
explanation: On-site Customer is an XP practice where a real customer (or representative) is available full-time to answer questions, set priorities, and provide feedback. This enables continuous clarification and reduces misunderstandings.

q: What does a 'burnup chart' show that a burndown chart does not?
options: The remaining work in the sprint
The total scope of work and how much has been completed, making scope changes visible
The number of defects discovered per day
The team's velocity over multiple sprints
explanation: A burnup chart shows both the total scope (top line) and the work completed (bottom line going up). This makes scope changes (scope creep) visible. A burndown chart only shows remaining work and does not clearly show scope changes.

q: The concept of 'Cumulative Flow Diagram' (CFD) is most associated with which Agile framework?
explanation: The Cumulative Flow Diagram (CFD) is a key analytics tool in Kanban. It shows the amount of work in each state (column) over time, helping teams visualize flow, identify bottlenecks, and measure cycle time and throughput.

q: Which of the following is NOT a role in the Scrum framework?
explanation: The three roles in Scrum are: Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team. 'Project Manager' is not a Scrum role. In Scrum, the responsibilities traditionally held by a project manager are distributed among these three roles.

q: In BDD, who is primarily responsible for writing the feature files with Given-When-Then scenarios?
options: Collaboration between business analysts, developers, and testers (Three Amigos)
explanation: BDD encourages collaboration between Business Analysts, Developers, and Testers â€” known as the 'Three Amigos'. They collaborate to create scenarios in Given-When-Then format, ensuring a shared understanding of requirements.

q: According to the Agile Manifesto, the most efficient and effective method of conveying information within a development team is:
explanation: Agile Manifesto Principle #6 states: 'The most efficient and effective method of conveying information to and within a development team is face-to-face conversation.' This emphasizes direct communication over written or tool-mediated communication.

q: Which of the following Agile Manifesto principles directly addresses technical quality?
options: Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances agility
The best architectures emerge from self-organizing teams
Simplicity â€” the art of maximizing the amount of work not done â€” is essential
explanation: Agile Manifesto Principle #9 states: 'Continuous attention to technical excellence and good design enhances agility.' This directly addresses the importance of technical quality in maintaining the ability to respond to change.

q: Which of the following is a functional requirement for a banking application?
options: The system shall allow users to transfer funds between accounts
The system shall respond to queries within 2 seconds
The system shall be available 99.9% of the time
The system shall support 10,000 concurrent users
explanation: Functional requirements describe what the system does â€” its features and behaviors. 'Allow users to transfer funds' is a specific system function. The other options describe performance, availability, and scalability â€” all non-functional requirements.

q: Which of the following best describes a non-functional requirement?
options: The system shall generate monthly salary reports
The system shall encrypt all user passwords using AES-256
The system shall process 500 transactions per second
The system shall allow admin users to add new employees
explanation: Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes like performance, security, reliability, etc. '500 transactions per second' is a performance (non-functional) requirement. The others describe system behaviors/functions.

q: The IEEE 830 standard is related to which software engineering document?
explanation: IEEE 830 (now superseded by IEEE 29148) defines the recommended practice for Software Requirements Specifications (SRS). It provides guidelines on structure, content, and quality of SRS documents.

q: According to IEEE 830, which of the following is NOT a recommended characteristic of a good SRS?
explanation: IEEE 830 states that a good SRS should be correct, unambiguous, complete, consistent, ranked for importance, verifiable, modifiable, and traceable. 'Ambiguous' is the opposite of what is desired â€” requirements must be unambiguous.

q: In the context of requirements engineering, what is 'elicitation'?
options: Writing formal specifications in a mathematical notation
The process of gathering requirements from stakeholders
Checking requirements for consistency and completeness
explanation: Requirements elicitation is the process of identifying, collecting, and discovering requirements from stakeholders through various techniques like interviews, workshops, and observation.

q: Which requirements elicitation technique involves watching end-users perform their actual work tasks in their natural environment?
explanation: Observation (also called ethnography or contextual inquiry) involves the analyst watching users perform their tasks in their real work environment. It is useful for discovering tacit knowledge that users may not articulate in interviews.

q: JAD sessions in requirements engineering stand for:
explanation: JAD stands for Joint Application Development. It is a structured workshop technique where developers, users, and stakeholders collaborate intensively to gather and agree on requirements, reducing development time and improving quality.

q: Which elicitation technique is most suitable when stakeholders are geographically dispersed and large in number?
explanation: Questionnaires/surveys are most suitable for large, geographically dispersed stakeholder groups because they can be distributed easily (even online), are cost-effective, and allow collection of standardized data from many respondents simultaneously.

q: A prototype built specifically to elicit requirements and then discarded is called:
explanation: A throwaway (or rapid/exploratory) prototype is built quickly to explore requirements and gather feedback from users, after which it is discarded. An evolutionary prototype is incrementally refined into the final system.

q: Which of the following is a primary advantage of using interviews as an elicitation technique?
options: They are inexpensive and require minimal analyst time
They allow rich, detailed exploration through follow-up questions
They are ideal for reaching hundreds of stakeholders simultaneously
They eliminate the need for documentation
explanation: Interviews allow analysts to probe deeply, ask follow-up questions, clarify ambiguities, and explore unanticipated areas. They provide rich qualitative information but are time-consuming and cannot easily scale to many stakeholders.

q: Requirements validation is performed to ensure that:
options: The requirements reflect what the system will actually be built to do
Requirements correctly represent the customer's actual needs
The development team understands the technical architecture
The project schedule and budget are feasible
explanation: Requirements validation checks that the documented requirements accurately reflect the stakeholders' real needs and intentions. It answers 'Are we building the right thing?' â€” distinct from verification which asks 'Are we building it right?'

q: Which of the following is a requirements validation technique?
explanation: Requirements validation techniques include reviews/walkthroughs, prototyping, test-case generation, and automated consistency checking. Walkthroughs involve stakeholders reviewing requirements documents to identify errors, omissions, and ambiguities.

q: A feasibility study conducted at the start of a project primarily assesses:
options: The detailed technical architecture of the system
Whether the proposed system is worth building given technical, financial, and organizational constraints
The exact list of all functional requirements
The testing strategy for the system
explanation: A feasibility study determines whether a proposed system is viable considering technical feasibility (can it be built?), economic feasibility (is it cost-effective?), organizational/operational feasibility (will it be used?), and schedule feasibility.

q: In a use case diagram, a 'generalization' relationship between two actors means:
options: One use case includes the behavior of another
One actor inherits the roles and capabilities of another actor
One use case extends another under certain conditions
Two use cases are mutually exclusive
explanation: Generalization between actors in UML means one actor (child) inherits the roles and associations of another actor (parent). For example, 'Premium Customer' generalizes 'Customer', inheriting all interactions the Customer has with the system.

q: In a use case diagram, the <<include>> relationship indicates:
options: A use case that optionally extends another
A use case whose behavior is always incorporated into the base use case
Two use cases that cannot occur simultaneously
An actor that specializes another actor
explanation: <<include>> means the base use case always incorporates the behavior of the included use case. It is used for mandatory sub-behaviors shared across multiple use cases (e.g., 'Login' is always included in 'View Account Balance').

q: The <<extend>> relationship in use case diagrams is used when:
options: A use case always calls another use case
A use case optionally adds behavior to a base use case under specific conditions
Two actors share the same use cases
A use case replaces another use case
explanation: <<extend>> represents optional or conditional extension of a base use case. The extending use case adds behavior at defined extension points only when certain conditions are met (e.g., 'Apply Discount' extends 'Process Payment' when a coupon is entered).

q: A use case diagram primarily captures:
options: The internal logic and algorithms of the system
The functional requirements from the user's perspective showing actors and their interactions
The database schema and entity relationships
The deployment infrastructure of the system
explanation: Use case diagrams are behavioral UML diagrams that capture functional requirements from the user's perspective. They show actors (users/external systems) and the use cases (system functions) they interact with, without detailing internal implementation.

q: In Agile development, a user story follows which template?
options: As a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]
explanation: The standard user story template is: 'As a [role/persona], I want [goal/desire], so that [benefit/value].' This format keeps requirements user-focused and communicates both the need and the business value.

q: User stories in Agile are assessed for completeness using the INVEST criteria. What does 'V' in INVEST stand for?
explanation: INVEST stands for: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable. 'V' is Valuable â€” every user story must deliver value to the user or business. Stories that are not valuable should be removed from the backlog.

q: A Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) is used to:
options: Track the project schedule and resource allocation
Map requirements to their source and to corresponding design, code, and test artifacts
explanation: An RTM links each requirement to its origin (stakeholder/document) and forward to design, code, and test cases. It ensures all requirements are addressed, no unauthorized features are added, and impact of changes can be analyzed.

q: In FURPS+, what does the letter 'F' represent?
explanation: FURPS+ stands for Functionality, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability, plus constraints (+). 'F' = Functionality covers the features and capabilities the system must provide (i.e., functional requirements).

q: In FURPS+, which category would 'the system must have an intuitive interface requiring no more than 2 hours of training' fall under?
explanation: FURPS+ 'U' = Usability covers ease of use, learnability, and user interface quality. Requirements about training time, user interface intuitiveness, and accessibility fall under usability.

q: In FURPS+, the '+' extends the model to include which additional categories?
options: Implementation, Interface, Operations, Packaging, and Legal constraints
explanation: The '+' in FURPS+ adds: Implementation constraints (programming languages, tools), Interface constraints (hardware/software interfaces), Operations constraints (system administration), Packaging constraints (delivery), and Legal constraints (licensing, compliance).

q: Which of the following is an example of a reliability requirement (the 'R' in FURPS+)?
options: The system shall support English and Hindi languages
The system shall have a mean time between failures (MTBF) of at least 1000 hours
The system shall respond to search queries in under 1 second
The system shall allow role-based access control
explanation: Reliability (R in FURPS+) covers availability, accuracy, recoverability, and MTBF. MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is a classic reliability metric. Response time is performance; language support is functionality; access control is functionality/security.

q: The SRS document is primarily written by:
options: Systems/requirements analysts in collaboration with stakeholders
explanation: The SRS is typically authored by systems analysts or requirements engineers in close collaboration with stakeholders (users, clients, domain experts). Developers use it as input for design, and customers review it to confirm their needs are captured.

q: Which section of an IEEE 830 SRS document describes the overall factors that affect the product and its requirements?
explanation: IEEE 830 SRS structure: Section 1 = Introduction, Section 2 = Overall Description (product perspective, functions, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions), Section 3 = Specific Requirements (detailed functional/non-functional requirements).

q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes functional from non-functional requirements?
options: Functional requirements describe system constraints; non-functional describe system behaviors
Functional requirements describe what the system does; non-functional describe how well it does it
Functional requirements are tested; non-functional requirements cannot be tested
Functional requirements are mandatory; non-functional requirements are optional
explanation: Functional requirements specify the functions/behaviors of a system (what it does). Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes â€” performance, security, usability, reliability â€” describing how well the system performs its functions.

q: Which of the following is an example of a performance non-functional requirement?
options: The system shall allow users to reset their password
The system shall load the home page within 3 seconds for 95% of requests
The system shall send email notifications on order placement
The system shall maintain an audit log of all transactions
explanation: Performance requirements specify measurable speed, throughput, or resource usage constraints. 'Load home page within 3 seconds for 95% of requests' is a clear performance requirement with measurable criteria.

q: The process of formally documenting requirements in an SRS is part of which requirements engineering activity?
explanation: Requirements engineering activities: Elicitation (gathering), Specification (documenting formally in SRS), Validation (checking correctness), and Management (handling changes). Writing the SRS is the specification activity.

q: In a feasibility study, 'technical feasibility' assesses:
options: Whether the project budget is adequate
Whether the required technology and expertise are available to build the system
Whether the organization is ready to adopt the new system
Whether the project can be completed within the deadline
explanation: Technical feasibility evaluates whether the necessary technology, tools, hardware, and skilled personnel exist to develop the proposed system. It answers: 'Can this be built with current or obtainable technology?'

q: Which of the following elicitation techniques is a formal workshop involving users, management, and developers working together to define requirements?
explanation: JAD is a structured group workshop facilitated by a trained facilitator, bringing together key stakeholders (users, managers, IT staff) to collaboratively define requirements. It reduces development time and conflicts by achieving consensus in workshops.

q: Which requirements elicitation technique is most effective for discovering requirements that users cannot easily articulate?
explanation: Observation is most effective for discovering tacit or implicit requirements â€” things users do automatically without thinking. Users often cannot articulate these practices in interviews because they are habitual or unconscious.

q: A use case description typically includes all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Main success scenario and alternative flows
explanation: A use case description includes: name, actors, preconditions, postconditions, main success scenario (normal flow), and alternative/exception flows. It does NOT include source code â€” that belongs to the design/implementation phase.

q: The 'acceptance criteria' in a user story serve the same purpose as which concept in traditional requirements engineering?
