explanation: Acceptance criteria define the conditions under which a user story is considered complete and correct â€” making the story verifiable/testable. This mirrors the IEEE 830 requirement that all requirements must be verifiable.

q: Forward traceability in an RTM means tracing requirements:
options: From design back to original stakeholder needs
From requirements forward to design, code, and test cases
From test cases back to defects found
From code modules back to requirements
explanation: Forward traceability traces from requirements forward to design, implementation, and test artifacts â€” ensuring every requirement is implemented and tested. Backward traceability goes from artifacts back to requirements.

q: Which of the following BEST describes the purpose of a Requirements Traceability Matrix?
options: To ensure the project stays within budget
To verify that all requirements have been implemented and tested
To assign tasks to development team members
explanation: RTM ensures complete coverage â€” every requirement is traced to at least one test case, verifying it has been implemented and can be tested. It also helps assess the impact of requirement changes.

q: In IEEE 830, the requirement 'The system should be user-friendly' is problematic because it violates which key characteristic?
explanation: 'User-friendly' is subjective and cannot be objectively measured or tested, violating verifiability. IEEE 830 states requirements must be verifiable â€” there must be a finite, cost-effective test to confirm the requirement is met.

q: Which of the following statements about the SRS document is TRUE according to IEEE 830?
options: The SRS should specify how the system will be implemented
The SRS should specify what the system should do, not how to implement it
The SRS is written primarily for the development team and not stakeholders
The SRS should include project schedule and cost estimates
explanation: IEEE 830 emphasizes that the SRS specifies external behavior (WHAT the system does) not internal implementation (HOW). It must be implementation-independent. Schedule and cost belong in the project plan, not the SRS.

q: The system boundary in a use case diagram represents:
options: The geographical limits of system deployment
The scope of the system â€” separating what is inside the system from external actors
The hardware limitations of the system
The security perimeter of the application
explanation: The system boundary (rectangle in use case diagram) defines the scope of the system being modeled. Use cases inside the boundary are functions the system performs; actors outside represent external users or systems that interact with it.

q: Which of the following is NOT a typical challenge in requirements engineering?
options: Stakeholders may not know what they want
Requirements may be incomplete or contradictory
The programming language has not been selected
Different stakeholders may have conflicting requirements
explanation: Requirements engineering challenges include: unclear/incomplete requirements, conflicting stakeholder needs, difficulty articulating needs, and changing requirements. The programming language choice is a design/implementation concern, not a requirements engineering challenge.

q: In FURPS+, 'Supportability' (the second 'S') primarily concerns:
options: The system's ability to handle increasing load
Ease of maintenance, testing, configuration, and installation
The security features of the system
explanation: Supportability in FURPS+ covers maintainability, testability, configurability, installability, and serviceability â€” essentially, how easy it is to maintain, update, and support the system after deployment.

q: An SRS document that has no conflicting requirements is said to be:
explanation: IEEE 830 defines consistency: an SRS is consistent if there are no conflicting requirements. Completeness means all relevant requirements are included. Correctness means each stated requirement is accurate.

q: A software project that uses prototyping for requirements elicitation would MOST benefit from:
options: Projects where requirements are well-understood and stable
Projects where users have difficulty articulating requirements and the domain is new
Projects with fixed-price contracts where no changes are expected
Projects that use formal mathematical specification
explanation: Prototyping is most beneficial when requirements are unclear or users cannot articulate their needs. A concrete prototype (UI mockup or working model) helps users react to something tangible and refine their actual requirements.

q: Which of the following correctly describes 'requirements creep'?
options: Requirements that are too detailed and technical
The gradual, uncontrolled expansion of project scope through continuous addition of new requirements
Requirements that are discovered only during testing
Requirements written in natural language that are difficult to understand
explanation: Requirements creep (or scope creep) is the continuous, uncontrolled addition of new requirements after the project has begun, without proper change control. It leads to project overruns in time and budget.

q: In Agile methodology, the product backlog is BEST described as:
options: A list of defects found during testing
A prioritized list of user stories and requirements for the product
The sprint schedule and team assignments
explanation: The product backlog is an ordered (prioritized) list of all desired work for the product, primarily in the form of user stories. It is maintained by the Product Owner and serves as the single source of requirements in Agile.

q: Which of the following use case diagram elements represents an external entity that interacts with the system?
explanation: In UML use case diagrams, actors (represented by stick figures) are external entities â€” human users or external systems â€” that interact with the system being modeled. They are always outside the system boundary.

q: Which of the following is an example of an 'operational feasibility' concern in a feasibility study?
options: The proposed system requires hardware that is too expensive
The technology needed for the system does not yet exist
Employees may resist adopting the new system due to training requirements
The project cannot be completed within the 6-month deadline
explanation: Operational feasibility (also called organizational feasibility) assesses whether the system will actually be used once deployed. Employee resistance, cultural fit, and training needs are operational feasibility concerns.

q: Requirements management involves all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Writing source code based on requirements
explanation: Requirements management covers: change control, version tracking, traceability maintenance, and conflict resolution. Writing source code is part of the implementation phase, not requirements management.

q: A 'domain requirement' in requirements engineering refers to:
options: Requirements from the IT infrastructure domain
Requirements derived from the application domain that reflect fundamental domain characteristics
Requirements that must be met for the system to be deployed on a network domain
Requirements specified by the project manager
explanation: Domain requirements originate from the application domain (e.g., banking, healthcare) and reflect fundamental domain rules, regulations, and practices. For example, in banking: 'Interest calculations must comply with RBI regulations.'

q: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between verification and validation in requirements engineering?
options: Verification checks requirements; validation checks code
Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?'; validation asks 'Are we building the right product?'
Verification is done by developers; validation is done by managers
Verification and validation mean the same thing in requirements engineering
explanation: Barry Boehm's classic distinction: Verification = 'Are we building the product right?' (conformance to specification), Validation = 'Are we building the right product?' (conformance to actual user needs). Requirements validation checks the latter.

q: In IEEE 830, the recommended approach for organizing the 'Specific Requirements' section is:
options: Can be organized by system mode, user class, object, feature, stimulus, or functional hierarchy
Must always be organized alphabetically by requirement name
Must follow the same structure as the use case diagram
explanation: IEEE 830 provides multiple valid organizational approaches for Section 3 (Specific Requirements): by system mode, user class, object, feature, stimulus, response, or functional hierarchy. The choice depends on the nature of the system.

q: Which design principle refers to the process of hiding the implementation details and showing only the essential features of an object?
explanation: Abstraction is the principle of hiding complex implementation details and exposing only the necessary interface. It allows designers to focus on what an object does rather than how it does it.

q: The degree to which the components of a software system are independent of each other is called:
explanation: Low coupling indicates that modules are largely independent of each other. This is a desirable property in software design as it makes modules easier to maintain, test, and reuse.

q: Which of the following is the STRONGEST (best) type of cohesion?
explanation: Functional cohesion is the strongest type of cohesion, where all elements of a module contribute to a single, well-defined task. It is the most desirable form of cohesion.

q: Which type of cohesion exists when all elements of a module are activated at the same time (e.g., initialization routines)?
explanation: Temporal cohesion occurs when elements are grouped because they are executed at the same time, such as startup initialization routines. Time of execution is what binds the elements together.

q: In which type of cohesion do elements of a module operate on the same input data or produce the same output data?
explanation: Communicational cohesion occurs when elements of a module operate on the same data set or contribute to activities that use the same input or output data.

q: Which type of cohesion is the WEAKEST and most undesirable?
explanation: Coincidental cohesion is the weakest form where elements are grouped arbitrarily with no meaningful relationship. It happens when module elements have no real relationship to each other.

q: Which type of cohesion occurs when the output of one element serves as input to the next element in sequence?
explanation: Sequential cohesion exists when elements are arranged so that output from one element becomes input to the next, similar to an assembly line or pipeline.

q: In procedural cohesion, elements are grouped because they:
options: Always execute together at a specific time
Follow a specific sequence of execution steps
explanation: Procedural cohesion groups elements that are executed in a particular order to perform a procedure, but do not necessarily share data. The control flow binds them together.

q: Which type of cohesion groups elements that perform similar functions but are selected by a control flag?
explanation: Logical cohesion groups elements that perform logically similar functions (e.g., all error-handling routines), where the actual function executed is selected by a control parameter.

q: Which is the WEAKEST (worst) form of coupling?
explanation: Content coupling is the worst (tightest) form of coupling where one module directly accesses or modifies the internal workings of another module. It creates strong interdependencies and should be avoided.

q: Which type of coupling is considered the BEST (loosest) and most desirable?
explanation: Data coupling is the best (loosest) form of coupling where modules share data through parameters (simple data items only). Each module operates only on what it needs and nothing more.

q: When two modules communicate by passing an entire data structure (record/object) but only use part of it, this is called:
explanation: Stamp coupling (also called structured data coupling) occurs when modules share a composite data structure (like a record or object) but only use parts of it, which may result in unnecessary data dependencies.

q: Which type of coupling occurs when one module controls the flow of another by passing a control flag or switch?
explanation: Control coupling occurs when one module passes a control element (flag, switch, or command) to another module that determines the logic flow or behavior of the receiving module.

q: Two modules sharing the same global data area is an example of:
explanation: Common coupling occurs when two or more modules share a global data area (e.g., global variables). Changes to global data can affect all modules using it, making maintenance difficult.

q: When modules depend on the same externally imposed data format, communication protocol, or device interface, it is called:
explanation: External coupling occurs when modules share an externally imposed format, protocol, or interface such as a file format, hardware interface, or communication protocol.

q: Which design principle suggests that a software system should be divided into separate components or units, each responsible for a specific functionality?
explanation: Modularity is the design principle that divides a system into distinct, manageable modules or components. Each module has a specific role, making the system easier to develop, test, and maintain.

q: In the MVC architectural pattern, what does 'M' stand for?
explanation: In MVC (Model-View-Controller), 'M' stands for Model, which represents the data and business logic of the application, independent of the user interface.

q: In the MVC design pattern, which component handles user input and updates the Model?
explanation: In MVC, the Controller handles user input, processes it, and updates the Model accordingly. It acts as an intermediary between the View and the Model.

q: Which architectural pattern replaces the Controller with a Presenter that handles all UI logic, and the View is passive?
explanation: MVP (Model-View-Presenter) replaces the Controller with a Presenter. The View is passive and delegates all user interaction handling to the Presenter, which also updates the View.

q: In MVVM architecture, what does 'VM' stand for?
explanation: In MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel), 'VM' stands for ViewModel. It acts as a bridge between the Model and View, exposing data streams and handling presentation logic with data binding.

q: Which architectural pattern organizes software into horizontal layers, each providing services to the layer above it?
explanation: The Layered (N-Tier) architectural pattern organizes code into layers where each layer provides specific services. Common layers include Presentation, Business Logic, and Data Access layers.

q: In a Client-Server architecture, which component provides resources or services?
explanation: In a Client-Server architecture, the Server is the component that provides resources, services, or data. The Client is the requester that consumes these services.

q: Which architectural style structures an application as a collection of small, independently deployable services?
explanation: Microservices architecture structures an application as a collection of small, loosely coupled, independently deployable services. Each service focuses on a specific business capability.

q: A key advantage of the Microservices architecture over Monolithic architecture is:
options: Services can be deployed and scaled independently
Simpler data management with shared database
explanation: A primary advantage of microservices is that individual services can be deployed, updated, and scaled independently without affecting the entire application, improving flexibility and resilience.

q: Which design notation uses rectangles to represent entities, diamonds for relationships, and ellipses for attributes?
explanation: An Entity-Relationship (ER) Diagram uses rectangles for entities, diamonds for relationships between entities, and ellipses (ovals) for attributes, to model data and their relationships.

q: Which design notation shows the flow of data through a system using processes, data stores, external entities, and data flows?
explanation: A Data Flow Diagram (DFD) represents the flow of data through a system. It uses four elements: processes (bubbles/circles), data stores, external entities (rectangles), and data flows (arrows).

q: In structured design, which diagram shows the hierarchical organization of program modules and their inter-relationships?
explanation: A Structure Chart (also called Module Structure Chart) is used in structured design to show the hierarchical decomposition of a system into modules and the relationships (calls and data flow) between them.

q: The concept of 'information hiding' in software design is closely related to which principle?
explanation: Information hiding, introduced by David Parnas, is closely related to abstraction and encapsulation. It involves hiding the internal design decisions of a module from other modules to reduce complexity and dependency.

q: Which of the following correctly ranks cohesion types from STRONGEST to WEAKEST?
options: Functional > Sequential > Communicational > Procedural > Temporal > Logical > Coincidental
Coincidental > Logical > Temporal > Procedural > Communicational > Sequential > Functional
Functional > Communicational > Sequential > Procedural > Temporal > Logical > Coincidental
Sequential > Functional > Communicational > Temporal > Procedural > Logical > Coincidental
explanation: The standard ranking of cohesion from strongest (best) to weakest (worst) is: Functional > Sequential > Communicational > Procedural > Temporal > Logical > Coincidental.

q: Which of the following correctly ranks coupling types from LOOSEST (best) to TIGHTEST (worst)?
options: Data > Stamp > Control > External > Common > Content
Content > Common > External > Control > Stamp > Data
Data > Control > Stamp > External > Common > Content
Stamp > Data > Control > Common > External > Content
explanation: The standard ranking of coupling from loosest (best) to tightest (worst) is: Data > Stamp > Control > External > Common > Content.

q: Which principle states that a module should be open for extension but closed for modification?
explanation: The Open/Closed Principle (OCP), part of SOLID principles, states that a software entity should be open for extension (new behavior can be added) but closed for modification (existing code should not change).

q: In MVC, what is the role of the View?
options: Handles business logic and data processing
Displays data from the Model to the user
Processes user input and updates the Model
explanation: In MVC, the View is responsible for displaying the data from the Model to the user. It is the presentation layer and renders the UI that the user interacts with.

q: Which type of coupling is present when module A directly reads or modifies the internal data of module B?
explanation: Content coupling is the tightest form of coupling where one module directly accesses, reads, or modifies the internal data or code of another module, violating encapsulation.

q: Fan-in and fan-out in structured design relate to:
options: The number of data items passed between modules
The number of modules calling a given module and the number of modules a given module calls
The depth of the module hierarchy
The cohesion level of a module
explanation: Fan-in is the number of modules that call a given module (a measure of reuse). Fan-out is the number of modules that a given module calls. High fan-out can indicate excessive control complexity.

q: The 'Presenter' in MVP differs from the 'Controller' in MVC in that:
options: Presenter is part of the Model layer
Presenter has no knowledge of the View
Presenter directly manipulates the View through an interface, making the View passive
explanation: In MVP, the Presenter handles all UI logic and directly updates the View through a defined interface. The View is passive and does not have direct access to the Model, unlike MVC where the View can observe the Model.

q: Which design pattern is particularly well-suited for data-binding-heavy frameworks like Angular and WPF?
explanation: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) is particularly suited for frameworks with powerful data-binding capabilities like Angular, WPF, and Knockout.js. The ViewModel exposes data observables that the View binds to automatically.

q: In a layered architecture, if the Presentation layer directly accesses the Data Access layer, bypassing the Business Logic layer, this violates:
explanation: In strict layered architecture, each layer should only communicate with adjacent layers. Bypassing the Business Logic layer violates layer isolation (or closed layer rule), creating unwanted dependencies.

q: Which UML diagram is most commonly used to represent the static structure of a system, showing classes, attributes, methods, and relationships?
explanation: The UML Class Diagram represents the static structure of a system by showing classes with their attributes and methods, and the relationships between them (inheritance, association, aggregation, composition).

q: A software module that calculates employee salary does exactly one thing and does it well. This is an example of:
explanation: Functional cohesion is when all elements of a module work together to perform a single, well-defined task. A salary calculation module that does exactly one function exemplifies functional cohesion.

q: The DFD (Data Flow Diagram) Level-0, which represents the entire system as a single process with its external entities, is called:
explanation: A Level-0 DFD, also known as a Context Diagram, shows the entire system as a single process bubble (black box) interacting with all external entities. It defines the system boundary.

q: Which of the following is a characteristic of good software design in terms of coupling and cohesion?
explanation: Good software design aims for low coupling (modules are independent of each other) and high cohesion (elements within a module are strongly related). This improves maintainability and reusability.

q: In structured design, the process of transforming a DFD into a structure chart is called:
explanation: Transform analysis (also called transform-centered design) is a structured design strategy that maps a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) into a structure chart by identifying the central transform of the data flow.

q: Which architectural design pattern separates concerns such that each microservice manages its own database?
explanation: The 'Database per service' pattern in microservices ensures each service has its own dedicated database, providing loose coupling and allowing each service to use the most appropriate database technology.

q: Which notation is used in Structure Charts to indicate that a module is called conditionally?
options: A diamond shape on the calling arc
A circle on the calling arc
explanation: In Structure Charts, a diamond (decision symbol) placed on the calling arc between two modules indicates a conditional call â€” the subordinate module is called only under certain conditions.

q: A module that groups together operations that must all occur when an error is detected (e.g., log error, notify user, clean up) exhibits which type of cohesion?
explanation: Temporal cohesion groups activities that need to be executed at the same time or in response to the same event (like error detection), even if the activities are not logically related to each other.

q: The principle that each module should have a single, well-defined responsibility is known as:
explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) states that a module or class should have one and only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one job or responsibility.

q: In an MVP architecture, who is responsible for fetching data from the Model and pushing it to the View?
explanation: In MVP, the Presenter is responsible for fetching data from the Model, applying any presentation logic, and then updating the View. The View has no direct interaction with the Model.

q: Which type of cohesion is demonstrated when a module validates input, then formats the data, then stores it â€” where each step's output feeds the next?
explanation: Sequential cohesion is demonstrated when the output of one activity within a module serves as input to the next activity. The validate â†’ format â†’ store pipeline is a classic example.

q: Which design notation uses nodes and directed edges to represent the control flow paths through a program, used to calculate cyclomatic complexity?
explanation: A Control Flow Graph (CFG) uses nodes to represent blocks of code and directed edges to represent possible control flow paths. It is the basis for calculating McCabe's cyclomatic complexity.

q: The concept of 'separation of concerns' in software design means:
options: Combining multiple functionalities into a single module for efficiency
Dividing a program into distinct sections each addressing a separate concern or responsibility
Ensuring all modules share the same global data
Designing all modules to perform similar functions
explanation: Separation of Concerns (SoC) is a design principle that advocates dividing a system into distinct sections, each addressing a specific concern (e.g., UI, business logic, data access), reducing complexity.

q: Which level of software testing focuses on testing individual components or modules in isolation?
explanation: Unit Testing focuses on testing the smallest testable parts (units/modules) of a software in isolation, verifying that each unit performs as designed.

q: In the V-Model, which testing level corresponds to the System Design phase?
explanation: In the V-Model, Integration Testing corresponds to the System Design phase. Unit Testing corresponds to Detailed Design, System Testing to System Requirements, and Acceptance Testing to Business Requirements.

q: Alpha testing is performed by:
options: End users at the customer site
Internal testers at the developer's site
An independent testing team in production
explanation: Alpha testing is performed by internal testers (QA team or employees) at the developer's site before the product is released to external users.

q: Beta testing is conducted by:
options: A selected group of end users in their own environment
explanation: Beta testing is performed by a selected group of real end users in their own environment (outside the developer's site) before the final product release.

q: Which type of testing verifies that the software meets the business requirements and is acceptable for delivery?
explanation: Acceptance Testing (User Acceptance Testing - UAT) is performed to verify that the software satisfies business requirements and is ready for delivery to end users.

q: Black-box testing is also known as:
explanation: Black-box testing is also known as behavioral or functional testing because it tests the software based on its behavior/functionality without knowledge of internal code structure.

q: In white-box testing, which of the following is NOT examined by the tester?
explanation: White-box testing examines internal structure, code logic, and branching conditions. Testing based solely on user requirements without looking at internal code is characteristic of black-box testing.

q: Grey-box testing combines features of:
explanation: Grey-box testing combines features of both black-box and white-box testing. The tester has partial knowledge of the internal workings while also testing from a behavioral/functional perspective.

q: Equivalence Partitioning divides input data into:
options: Valid and invalid partitions that should be treated the same
explanation: Equivalence Partitioning divides the input domain into classes/partitions where all values in a partition are expected to produce the same result, reducing the number of test cases needed.

q: For a field that accepts values between 1 and 100 (inclusive), Boundary Value Analysis would test which values?
explanation: Boundary Value Analysis tests at the edges: just below the lower boundary (0), at the lower boundary (1), just above it (2), just below the upper boundary (99), at the upper boundary (100), and just above it (101).

q: A Decision Table is best suited for testing systems that have:
options: Complex combinations of conditions and actions
explanation: Decision Tables are best suited for systems with complex business logic involving multiple conditions and their corresponding actions/outcomes â€” each column represents a unique combination.

q: State Transition Testing is most applicable to systems that:
options: Exhibit different behavior based on their current state and inputs
Are purely functional with no state
explanation: State Transition Testing is used for systems where behavior depends on the current state and input events, such as embedded systems, workflow engines, or ATM machines.

q: Which white-box coverage criterion requires that every executable statement in the code is executed at least once?
explanation: Statement Coverage (also called Line Coverage) requires that every executable statement in the program is executed at least once during testing.

q: Branch Coverage ensures that:
options: Every decision outcome (true/false) is tested at least once
All paths through the program are tested
explanation: Branch Coverage (Decision Coverage) requires that every branch (true and false outcome) of every decision/control structure is executed at least once.

q: Which coverage criterion is the most comprehensive and hardest to achieve in practice?
explanation: Path Coverage requires testing every possible path through the program. This is the most thorough but impractical for programs with loops or complex logic due to exponential path explosion.

q: Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC) is primarily required in which domain?
explanation: MC/DC is mandated by aviation standards (DO-178C) for safety-critical software. It requires each condition in a decision to independently affect the decision outcome.

q: Regression Testing is performed to ensure that:
options: Previously working functionality is not broken by new changes
The system meets user acceptance criteria
explanation: Regression Testing verifies that recent code changes (bug fixes, new features, or enhancements) have not adversely affected existing functionality.

q: Smoke Testing is also known as:
explanation: Smoke Testing is also called Build Verification Testing. It is a preliminary test to check the basic functionality of a new build before detailed testing begins.

q: What is the primary purpose of Smoke Testing?
options: To test all functionalities in depth
To verify that the critical functions of a build work before further testing
explanation: Smoke Testing checks whether the most critical functionalities of a build are working correctly. If it fails, the build is rejected and returned for fixes before detailed testing.

q: Sanity Testing is performed to:
options: Test the entire application after a new build
Verify a specific functionality or bug fix after a change
explanation: Sanity Testing is a narrow regression test focused on verifying that a specific bug fix or new functionality works correctly. It is less comprehensive than full regression testing.

q: Performance Testing is a type of:
explanation: Performance Testing is a non-functional testing type that evaluates how well a system performs under specific conditions (speed, scalability, stability, resource usage).

q: Load Testing determines the system's behavior under:
explanation: Load Testing evaluates system behavior under expected and peak (anticipated maximum) load conditions to identify performance bottlenecks.

q: Stress Testing differs from Load Testing in that it:
options: Tests the system beyond its normal operational capacity to find breaking point
explanation: Stress Testing pushes the system beyond its designed capacity to determine how it fails and recovers. Load Testing tests within expected capacity; Stress Testing goes beyond it.

q: Verification in software testing refers to:
options: Are we building the right product?
Are we building the product right?
Does the product meet user needs?
explanation: Verification checks that the software is being built correctly according to specifications and design documents (process-oriented). The question is 'Are we building the product right?'

q: Validation in software testing refers to:
options: Are we building the product right?
Does the code compile without errors?
Are we building the right product?
explanation: Validation checks that the final software product meets the actual user needs and requirements (product-oriented). The question is 'Are we building the right product?'

q: Which of the following is an example of Verification activity?
explanation: Verification activities include reviews, walkthroughs, and inspections of documents and code. Validation activities involve actual execution of the software (testing).

q: Integration testing that starts with testing the lowest-level modules first is called:
explanation: Bottom-up integration testing starts with the lowest-level (leaf) modules and progressively integrates higher-level modules. Stubs are not needed but test drivers are required.

q: In Top-down integration testing, what are used to simulate lower-level modules not yet developed?
explanation: In Top-down integration testing, Stubs are used as substitutes for lower-level modules that have not yet been developed or integrated.

q: In Bottom-up integration testing, what are used to simulate higher-level modules?
explanation: In Bottom-up integration testing, Drivers are used as substitutes for higher-level modules that call the modules being tested.

q: Big Bang integration testing involves:
options: Testing modules one at a time
Integrating and testing all modules at once
explanation: Big Bang integration testing integrates all or most modules simultaneously and tests them together. It is simple but difficult to isolate defects when failures occur.

q: Which of the following best describes Equivalence Partitioning?
options: Dividing input data into classes where behavior is expected to be the same
explanation: Equivalence Partitioning divides the input domain into equivalence classes where all values within a class are expected to produce the same type of result, reducing redundant test cases.

q: For a login field accepting usernames of 5 to 10 characters, how many equivalence partitions exist?
explanation: There are 3 partitions: invalid (less than 5 chars), valid (5 to 10 chars), and invalid (more than 10 chars). One valid partition and two invalid partitions.

q: Which testing technique is used when testing a system's response to state changes, such as an ATM machine?
explanation: State Transition Testing models and tests systems that change behavior based on current state and events â€” like an ATM machine that changes between idle, card inserted, PIN entered, and transaction states.

q: What is the minimum number of test cases required for 100% statement coverage in a straight-line program (no branches)?
explanation: A straight-line program with no branches requires only 1 test case to achieve 100% statement coverage since execution follows a single path through all statements.

q: Condition Coverage requires that:
options: Each decision has both true and false outcomes tested
Each individual Boolean condition within a decision evaluates to both true and false
explanation: Condition Coverage (Simple Condition Coverage) requires that each individual boolean sub-expression (condition) within each decision evaluates to both true and false at least once.

q: Which of the following is a key difference between Smoke and Sanity Testing?
options: Smoke tests the entire app; Sanity tests a specific feature/fix
Sanity is done first; Smoke is done after
Smoke is white-box; Sanity is black-box
explanation: Smoke Testing is a broad, shallow test of the entire application's critical functions after a new build. Sanity Testing is a narrow, deep test of a specific functionality or bug fix.

q: Soak Testing (Endurance Testing) is a type of performance testing that verifies:
options: System stability and memory leaks under sustained load over a long period
explanation: Soak/Endurance Testing runs the system under a significant load for an extended period to detect memory leaks, performance degradation, and stability issues over time.

q: Spike Testing evaluates the system's response to:
options: Sudden and extreme increases in load
explanation: Spike Testing checks how the system behaves when subjected to sudden, dramatic increases in load/traffic â€” testing whether it recovers gracefully after the spike.

q: The primary goal of System Testing is to:
options: Validate the complete integrated system against specified requirements
explanation: System Testing validates the fully integrated software system against its specified functional and non-functional requirements. It is performed on the complete system.

q: Which testing type is performed without executing the program's code?
explanation: Static Testing (reviews, walkthroughs, inspections) is performed without executing the code. Dynamic Testing involves actual execution of the software.

q: In the context of Boundary Value Analysis, for the range [10, 20], which set of values is most appropriate?
explanation: BVA tests values at boundaries and just outside them: 9 (just below lower), 10 (lower boundary), 11 (just above lower), 19 (just below upper), 20 (upper boundary), 21 (just above upper).

q: Which of the following statements about grey-box testing is TRUE?
options: Tester has complete knowledge of source code
Tester has no knowledge of internal structure
Tester has partial knowledge of internal structure
It is performed only at the unit level
explanation: In Grey-box testing, the tester has partial knowledge of the internal workings (e.g., database schema, architecture) but tests from the user's perspective, combining black-box and white-box approaches.

q: Acceptance Testing that is mandated by contract or regulations is called:
explanation: Contract Acceptance Testing is performed against criteria defined in a contract. Regulatory Acceptance Testing checks compliance with regulations. Both are formal types of acceptance testing.

q: Which coverage metric subsumes (is stronger than) statement coverage?
explanation: Branch Coverage subsumes (is stronger than) Statement Coverage â€” if all branches are covered, all statements are automatically covered. However, the reverse is not true.

q: The cyclomatic complexity of a program determines the minimum number of test cases required for:
explanation: Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G) = E - N + 2P) gives the number of linearly independent paths (basis paths) in a program, which is the minimum number of test cases for path coverage.

q: Which testing approach checks only the critical paths and core functionalities after a bug fix, rather than the full regression suite?
explanation: Sanity Testing is a subset of regression testing that focuses on verifying that specific functionality works after a code change, without testing the entire application.

q: In Decision Table testing, a column represents:
options: A unique combination of conditions and their resulting actions
explanation: Each column in a decision table represents one rule â€” a unique combination of condition values and the corresponding action(s) to be taken.

q: For a program with cyclomatic complexity of 5, what is the minimum number of test cases needed to achieve basis path coverage?
explanation: Cyclomatic complexity directly gives the number of linearly independent paths (basis paths). A cyclomatic complexity of 5 means a minimum of 5 test cases are needed for basis path coverage.

q: Which of the following is NOT a level of software testing?
explanation: Unit, Integration, System, and Acceptance are the four main levels of software testing. Mutation Testing is a technique (a type/method) used to evaluate the quality of test cases, not a testing level.

q: Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT) primarily focuses on:
options: System operational aspects like backup, recovery, and maintainability
explanation: Operational Acceptance Testing verifies that the system is ready for operational use, checking backup/restore procedures, disaster recovery, maintenance tasks, and operational workflows.

q: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between Verification and Validation (V&V)?
options: Verification is done after deployment; Validation is done during development
Verification ensures the product is built right; Validation ensures the right product is built
Both are exactly the same process
Validation uses only static techniques; Verification uses only dynamic techniques
explanation: Verification (Are we building the product right?) checks conformance to specifications. Validation (Are we building the right product?) checks that the product meets actual user needs. Both are distinct but complementary activities.

q: Which software metric measures the number of executable source statements in a program?
explanation: Lines of Code (LOC) is a size metric that counts the number of source lines (executable statements) in a program. It is one of the earliest and simplest software size metrics.

q: In Function Point Analysis, which of the following is NOT one of the five information domain values?
explanation: The five information domain values are: External Inputs, External Outputs, External Inquiries, Internal Logical Files, and External Interface Files. Cyclomatic Paths is not a Function Point domain value.

q: McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity V(G) for a control flow graph with E edges, N nodes, and P connected components is:
explanation: McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity formula is V(G) = E - N + 2P, where E = edges, N = nodes, P = connected components (usually 1). This measures the number of linearly independent paths through the program.

q: In Basic COCOMO, the effort equation for an Organic mode project is:
explanation: Basic COCOMO Organic mode: Effort (PM) = 2.4 Ã— (KLOC)^1.05. Semi-detached: 3.0 Ã— (KLOC)^1.12. Embedded: 3.6 Ã— (KLOC)^1.20. Organic has the smallest coefficient and exponent reflecting smaller, well-understood projects.

q: Which COCOMO project type is characterized by a small team with good experience working with well-understood application requirements?
explanation: Organic mode in COCOMO describes small-to-medium software projects where teams have good experience with similar systems and requirements are relatively well understood, resulting in lower effort estimates.

q: The Embedded mode in COCOMO applies to projects that:
options: Involve small teams working on simple batch problems
Have a mix of experienced and inexperienced staff
Must operate within tight hardware, software, and operational constraints
Are government projects with flexible timelines
explanation: Embedded mode in COCOMO applies to projects that must operate within tight constraints on hardware, software, and operational procedures â€” such as real-time systems, flight control, or ATM software.

q: In the Intermediate COCOMO model, effort is calculated using:
options: KLOC multiplied by an Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF) from 15 cost driver ratings
explanation: Intermediate COCOMO refines Basic COCOMO by multiplying the nominal effort (from KLOC) by an EAF that is the product of 15 cost driver attribute ratings covering product, hardware, personnel, and project factors.

q: Halstead's program vocabulary (Î·) is defined as:
options: Î·1 + Î·2, where Î·1 = unique operators, Î·2 = unique operands
N1 + N2, where N1 = total operators, N2 = total operands
explanation: Halstead program vocabulary Î· = Î·1 + Î·2, where Î·1 is the number of distinct (unique) operators and Î·2 is the number of distinct (unique) operands. Program length N = N1 + N2 counts all occurrences.

q: Halstead's Volume (V) is calculated as:
explanation: Halstead Volume V = N Ã— log2(Î·), where N = N1 + N2 is the program length (total operators + operands) and Î· = Î·1 + Î·2 is the vocabulary. It measures the information content of the program.

q: Defect Density is defined as:
options: Number of defects per person-month of effort
Number of defects per KLOC of delivered software
Number of defects found per testing hour
Ratio of defects found in testing to defects injected in coding
explanation: Defect Density = Confirmed defects / Size (KLOC). It measures the concentration of defects in a software product and is a key software quality metric used to benchmark product quality.

q: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is related to MTTF and MTTR by the formula:
explanation: MTBF = MTTF + MTTR. MTTF is the average time from repair to next failure; MTTR is the average repair time. Together they span the full failure-to-failure cycle, which is MTBF.

q: System Availability in terms of reliability metrics is expressed as:
explanation: Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR). It is the proportion of time a system is operational. Higher MTBF (less frequent failures) and lower MTTR (faster repairs) increase availability.

q: The Jelinski-Moranda software reliability model assumes:
options: Failure rate increases as faults are removed
Failure rate is constant throughout software life
Failure rate is proportional to remaining faults and drops by a constant after each fix
Failure rate follows a log-normal distribution
explanation: Jelinski-Moranda assumes the failure rate is proportional to the number of remaining faults. Each time a fault is detected and removed, the failure rate decreases by a constant amount Ï†, creating a step-wise decreasing failure intensity.

q: Which software reliability model uses a Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process (NHPP)?
explanation: The Goel-Okumoto model is an NHPP-based reliability model. It assumes the cumulative number of failures follows a Poisson process with an exponential mean value function m(t) = a(1 - e^(-bt)).

q: In Function Point Analysis, the Unadjusted Function Point count is adjusted using:
explanation: Adjusted FP = UFP Ã— VAF. The VAF is derived from 14 General System Characteristics (GSCs), each rated 0-5, giving VAF = 0.65 + 0.01 Ã— sum(GSCs). VAF ranges from 0.65 to 1.35.

q: A program has 10 nodes and 12 edges in its control flow graph with one connected component. Its cyclomatic complexity is:
explanation: V(G) = E - N + 2P = 12 - 10 + 2Ã—1 = 4. With 4 independent paths, this module is manageable (below McCabe's recommended max of 10).

q: McCabe recommends that the cyclomatic complexity of a module should not exceed:
explanation: McCabe's guideline is that cyclomatic complexity should not exceed 10 per module. Complexity above 10 indicates the module is too complex, difficult to test comprehensively, and prone to defects.

q: In Basic COCOMO, the Development Time (TDEV) for the Organic mode is:
explanation: Basic COCOMO development time for Organic mode: TDEV = 2.5 Ã— (PM)^0.35, where PM is effort in person-months. Semi-detached uses exponent 0.35, Embedded uses 0.32. TDEV gives calendar months.

q: Which characteristic best describes the Semi-detached mode in COCOMO?
options: Very small teams with excellent domain knowledge and no constraints
Tight hardware real-time constraints with strict reliability requirements
Mix of experienced and inexperienced staff with intermediate constraints
Large teams working on simple well-understood batch systems
explanation: Semi-detached mode represents projects with mixed-experience teams, some novel aspects, and intermediate levels of innovation and constraints â€” sitting between the easy Organic and difficult Embedded extremes.

q: Halstead's Difficulty (D) metric is calculated as:
explanation: Halstead Difficulty D = (Î·1/2) Ã— (N2/Î·2). It measures how difficult the program is to write or understand â€” proportional to unique operators and the ratio of total-to-unique operands.

q: Halstead's Effort metric (E) represents:
options: Time in hours to implement the program
Mental effort required, calculated as E = D Ã— V
Number of test cases needed for full branch coverage
Total lines of code written per developer per day
explanation: Halstead Effort E = D Ã— V (Difficulty Ã— Volume). It represents the number of elementary mental discriminations required to implement or comprehend the program, serving as a cognitive complexity measure.

q: In Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), the lowest level of decomposition is called a:
explanation: The lowest-level element in a WBS is a Work Package. It is small enough to be assigned, estimated, scheduled, and controlled. Work packages can be further broken into activities for scheduling.

q: A Gantt chart primarily shows:
options: Task durations and schedules plotted against a timeline
explanation: A Gantt chart is a horizontal bar chart with tasks on the Y-axis and time on the X-axis. Bars show each task's duration, start date, and end date, providing a visual project schedule for tracking progress.

q: In PERT analysis, the Expected Time (TE) for an activity with optimistic (O), most likely (M), and pessimistic (P) estimates is:
explanation: PERT expected time TE = (O + 4M + P) / 6. The most likely estimate M receives 4Ã— the weight of O and P. This beta-distribution-based weighted average accounts for uncertainty in activity duration.

q: The Critical Path in a project network is defined as:
options: The path with the maximum number of activities
The shortest path from start to finish
The longest path from start to finish that determines minimum project duration
The path with the highest risk activities
explanation: The Critical Path is the longest duration path through the project network. It determines the earliest possible project completion date. Activities on this path have zero float â€” any delay extends the project.

q: Total Float (Slack) of an activity in CPM is defined as:
options: Extra resources available for the activity
Time the activity can be delayed without delaying the project end date
Difference between optimistic and pessimistic time estimates
Buffer added to the critical path for safety
explanation: Total Float = Late Start - Early Start = Late Finish - Early Finish. It is the maximum time an activity can be delayed without affecting the project's overall completion date. Critical path activities have zero float.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about the critical path in a project network?
options: There can be only one critical path in any project
Multiple critical paths can exist if they share the same longest duration
The critical path is always the path with the fewest activities
The critical path eliminates the need for resource planning
explanation: A project can have multiple critical paths when two or more paths have identical longest durations. All such paths have zero float, and a delay on any activity on any of these paths delays the project.

q: In a PERT network, the variance of an activity's duration is calculated as:
explanation: PERT variance ÏƒÂ² = [(P - O) / 6]Â² = (P - O)Â²/36, where P = pessimistic time and O = optimistic time. Standard deviation Ïƒ = (P - O)/6. The variance of the critical path is the sum of variances of its activities.

q: The primary advantage of Function Points over LOC as a software size metric is:
options: Easier to count during implementation phase
Language-independent measure of functionality from the user's perspective
More precise at estimating testing effort specifically
Directly maps to COCOMO II cost drivers
explanation: Function Points are language-independent â€” the same functionality has the same FP count regardless of implementation language. LOC varies by language (e.g., the same system may need 10x more lines in assembly than in Python).

q: Halstead's estimated number of bugs (B) in a program is given by:
explanation: Halstead's bug estimate B = E^(2/3) / 3000, where E is the Halstead Effort. This provides a prediction of the number of latent bugs/errors in the program based on its complexity.

q: PERT was originally developed for:
options: US Navy Polaris submarine missile program
explanation: PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) was developed in 1957 by the US Navy's Special Projects Office for the Polaris Fleet Ballistic Missile program to manage its complex, uncertain schedule. CPM was developed independently by DuPont.

q: In CPM, the Early Finish (EF) of an activity is calculated as:
explanation: Early Finish = Early Start + Duration (EF = ES + D). The forward pass through the network computes ES and EF for each activity, starting from the project's start. The critical path has EF = LF for all its activities.

q: In Intermediate COCOMO, the Effort Adjustment Factor (EAF) is the product of ratings for how many cost drivers?
explanation: Intermediate COCOMO has 15 cost driver attributes: 4 product attributes (RELY, DATA, CPLX, STOR), 2 hardware attributes (TIME, TURN), 5 personnel attributes (ACAP, AEXP, PCAP, VEXP, LEXP), and 4 project attributes (MODP, TOOL, SCED). EAF = product of all 15.

q: Detailed COCOMO differs from Intermediate COCOMO primarily in that it:
options: Uses fewer cost driver attributes for simplicity
Does not use KLOC as the size measure
Applies cost driver ratings at the module and phase level rather than project level
Replaces cost drivers with Monte Carlo risk simulation
explanation: Detailed COCOMO provides phase-sensitive estimates by applying cost driver ratings individually at the module level and for each development phase (design, code, test), giving finer-grained effort distribution across the project.

q: The Musa Basic Execution Time reliability model measures software time in:
explanation: The Musa model uses CPU execution time rather than calendar time, normalizing for differences in processor speed. This makes reliability measurements consistent across different hardware environments.

q: A software project has 150 defects in a 50 KLOC system. The defect density is:
explanation: Defect Density = Total Defects / Size (KLOC) = 150 / 50 = 3 defects/KLOC. This metric is used to compare product quality across releases and projects, with lower values indicating higher quality.

q: In WBS, the '100% rule' states that:
options: 100% of team members must approve each work package
The WBS must capture 100% of the project scope â€” all deliverables and work, no more and no less
All work packages must be 100% complete before moving to the next phase
Each work package must be assigned to exactly one 100%-dedicated resource
explanation: The 100% rule ensures the WBS includes all project scope (nothing omitted) and nothing outside the scope (no gold-plating). The sum of child elements must equal 100% of the parent element's scope.

q: Halstead's Time (T) to implement a program is estimated as:
explanation: Halstead's programming time T = E / 18 seconds, where E is the Halstead Effort and 18 is the Stroud number (elementary mental discriminations per second). This converts the cognitive effort metric into estimated time.

q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes CPM from PERT?
options: CPM is used for R&D projects; PERT for construction projects
CPM uses single deterministic time estimates; PERT uses three probabilistic time estimates
PERT identifies the critical path; CPM does not
CPM uses resource leveling; PERT uses cost crashing
explanation: CPM uses a single deterministic time estimate per activity (focusing on cost-time trade-offs for repetitive projects). PERT uses three-point estimates (O, M, P) to handle schedule uncertainty in novel projects like R&D.

q: In Function Point Analysis, External Interface Files (EIF) are:
options: Files maintained by the application being measured
Files referenced by the application but maintained by another application
API endpoints exposed by the system
explanation: EIFs are data stores referenced (read) by the application being measured but created, updated, and deleted by another application. They contrast with ILFs (Internal Logical Files), which are maintained by the system itself.

q: The Basic COCOMO formula for Embedded mode is: Effort = 3.6 Ã— (KLOC)^1.20. For a 10 KLOC system, the estimated effort is approximately:
explanation: Effort = 3.6 Ã— (10)^1.20 = 3.6 Ã— 15.85 â‰ˆ 57 person-months. (10)^1.20 = 10 Ã— 10^0.20 = 10 Ã— 1.585 â‰ˆ 15.85. This higher estimate reflects the complexity of embedded/real-time systems.

q: McCabe's cyclomatic complexity can also be computed as:
options: Number of loops in the program only
Number of nodes in the flow graph
explanation: V(G) = Number of predicate nodes (binary decisions: IF, WHILE, FOR, CASE, etc.) + 1. This alternative formula counts all two-way branching points and adds 1, yielding the same result as E - N + 2P.

q: In software reliability, MTTR measures:
options: Mean Time To Release a software version
Mean Time To Repair a failed system and restore it to operation
Maximum Throughput To Recover from overload
Minimum Time To Respond to a user request
explanation: MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) is the average time required to detect, diagnose, and repair a failure and restore the system to operational status. Lower MTTR indicates better maintainability.

q: Which of the following is NOT a Halstead software science metric?
explanation: Halstead metrics include Volume, Difficulty, Effort, Length, Vocabulary, Time, and Bugs. Cyclomatic Number V(G) is McCabe's structural complexity metric based on control flow graphs, not a Halstead metric.

q: In a project schedule, Free Float of an activity is defined as:
options: Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project end date
Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the Early Start of any successor
Maximum slack available across all paths through the activity
Difference between Late Finish and Early Start of the activity
explanation: Free Float = min(ES of successors) - EF of the activity. It is the time an activity can be delayed without impacting any immediate successor's earliest start. Free Float â‰¤ Total Float for any activity.

q: The primary purpose of a Gantt chart in software project management is:
options: Visualizing task scheduling, durations, and progress against a timeline
Calculating function points and size estimates
explanation: A Gantt chart is a visual scheduling and tracking tool showing tasks, durations, start/end dates, dependencies, and completion status on a timeline. It helps managers plan, coordinate resources, and track progress.

q: In the Goel-Okumoto NHPP reliability model, the expected number of failures by time t is:
explanation: The Goel-Okumoto model has mean value function m(t) = a(1 - e^(-bt)), where 'a' is the expected total number of failures and 'b' is the fault detection rate. It models an S-curve-like cumulative failure pattern.

q: Which of the following metrics directly measures software testability complexity by counting the minimum number of test cases for complete branch coverage?
explanation: Cyclomatic Complexity V(G) equals the minimum number of independent test cases needed for complete branch coverage (basis path testing). Each independent path through the code requires at least one test case.

q: In project planning, a milestone on a Gantt chart represents:
options: A task with the longest duration on the critical path
A significant event or checkpoint with zero duration
The total budget allocated to a phase
A parallel set of tasks that can be executed simultaneously
explanation: A milestone is a significant project event (e.g., phase completion, delivery, approval) represented on a Gantt chart as a zero-duration marker (typically a diamond symbol). It marks progress checkpoints.

q: The Halstead metric that estimates the conceptual understanding required by a programmer is:
explanation: Halstead Difficulty (D) estimates how difficult a program is to understand or write. High D means more effort is needed to comprehend the code. D = (Î·1/2) Ã— (N2/Î·2) â€” programs that reuse few unique operands have high difficulty.

q: In software quality, which metric is used to quantify the rate of defects relative to software size for benchmarking?
explanation: Defect Density (defects/KLOC) normalizes defect counts by software size, enabling fair quality comparisons across projects, teams, and releases regardless of system size. It is a standard IEEE software quality metric.

q: Which ISO standard defines the software product quality model with six main quality characteristics including Functionality, Reliability, Usability, Efficiency, Maintainability, and Portability?
explanation: ISO 9126 defines the software product quality model with six main characteristics: Functionality, Reliability, Usability, Efficiency, Maintainability, and Portability. It was later superseded by ISO 25010.

q: ISO/IEC 25010 replaced ISO 9126 and expanded the quality model to how many quality characteristics?
explanation: ISO/IEC 25010 expanded the quality model from 6 (ISO 9126) to 8 characteristics: Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, and Portability.

q: Which quality characteristic in ISO 25010 refers to the degree to which a product or system can be used by people with the widest range of characteristics and capabilities?
explanation: Usability in ISO 25010 refers to the degree to which a product or system can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a specified context of use.

q: In ISO 25010, which sub-characteristic of Reliability refers to the degree to which a system continues to perform its required functions under stated conditions for a specified period?
explanation: Maturity is a sub-characteristic of Reliability in ISO 25010 that refers to the degree to which a system meets needs for reliability under normal operation.

q: Which quality attribute in ISO 9126/25010 measures the degree to which a software product can be transferred from one environment to another?
explanation: Portability is the quality characteristic that measures the degree to which a system, product, or component can be transferred from one hardware, software, or other operational or usage environment to another.

q: CMM stands for:
explanation: CMM stands for Capability Maturity Model, developed by the Software Engineering Institute (SEI) at Carnegie Mellon University to assess and improve software development processes.

q: At which CMM level are processes characterized as ad hoc and chaotic, with success depending on individual heroics?
explanation: CMM Level 1 (Initial) is characterized by ad hoc and chaotic processes. Success depends on individual effort and heroics rather than defined processes. There are no stable environments.

q: At CMM Level 2, processes are characterized as:
explanation: CMM Level 2 is called 'Managed' or 'Repeatable'. Basic project management processes are established to track cost, schedule, and functionality. Process discipline ensures that existing practices are retained during times of stress.

q: Which CMM level focuses on quantitative management of software processes using statistical and other quantitative techniques?
explanation: CMM Level 4 (Quantitatively Managed) focuses on quantitative management. Organizations set quantitative quality goals and use statistical process control to measure and manage software processes.

q: At CMMI Level 5 (Optimizing), the focus is on:
options: Continuous process improvement using quantitative feedback
explanation: CMMI Level 5 (Optimizing) focuses on continuous process improvement. Organizations use quantitative feedback from processes and from testing innovative ideas to drive continuous improvement.

q: Which CMMI level is associated with the process areas of Organizational Process Focus, Organizational Process Definition, and Integrated Project Management?
explanation: CMMI Level 3 (Defined) includes process areas like Organizational Process Focus, Organizational Process Definition, Integrated Project Management, Risk Management, and Decision Analysis and Resolution.

q: CMMI stands for:
explanation: CMMI stands for Capability Maturity Model Integration. It integrates previously separate models (SW-CMM, SECM, IPD-CMM) into a single improvement framework.

q: ISO 9001 is primarily a standard for:
explanation: ISO 9001 is an international standard for Quality Management Systems (QMS). It specifies requirements for organizations that want to demonstrate their ability to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and regulatory requirements.

q: The PDCA cycle in quality management stands for:
explanation: PDCA stands for Plan-Do-Check-Act (also known as Deming cycle or Shewhart cycle). It is an iterative four-step management method used in quality management for continuous improvement.

q: Six Sigma aims to reduce defects to fewer than how many defects per million opportunities (DPMO)?
explanation: Six Sigma aims to reduce process defects to fewer than 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO). The term 'Six Sigma' comes from the statistical concept that 6 standard deviations (sigma) from the mean results in this defect rate.

q: Which Six Sigma methodology is used for improving existing processes?
explanation: DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) is the Six Sigma methodology used for improving existing processes. DMADV (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify) is used for creating new processes or products.

q: In Six Sigma, what does DMADV stand for?
explanation: DMADV stands for Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify. It is the Six Sigma methodology used for designing new products or processes, also known as DFSS (Design for Six Sigma).

q: In Software Configuration Management (SCM), a Configuration Item (CI) is best described as:
options: Any hardware component of the system
An aggregation of hardware or software designated for configuration management
explanation: A Configuration Item (CI) is an aggregation of hardware and/or software, or any of its discrete portions, that satisfies an end-use function and is designated for configuration management. It can be hardware, software, documentation, or a combination.

q: A baseline in Software Configuration Management is:
options: The initial version of code before any changes
A formally approved version of a configuration item that serves as a basis for future development
A backup copy of all project files
explanation: A baseline is a formally approved version of a configuration item that serves as the basis for further development and can be changed only through formal change control procedures. Examples include functional baseline, allocated baseline, and product baseline.

q: Which of the following is NOT a primary activity of Software Configuration Management?
explanation: The primary activities of SCM are: Configuration Identification, Configuration Control, Configuration Status Accounting, and Configuration Auditing. Software Testing is a quality assurance activity, not an SCM activity.

q: In Git, which command is used to create a new branch and switch to it in a single step (modern syntax)?
explanation: Both 'git checkout -b <branch>' and 'git switch -c <branch>' create a new branch and switch to it. The 'git switch -c' is the newer, more explicit command introduced in Git 2.23, while 'git checkout -b' is the traditional way.

q: What does the Git command 'git commit --amend' do?
options: Creates a new commit on top of the current one
explanation: 'git commit --amend' modifies the most recent commit. It allows you to change the commit message, add new files, or make other changes to the last commit. It rewrites the commit history, so it should not be used on public/shared branches.

q: Which Git command integrates changes from one branch into another by replaying commits on top of the target branch?
explanation: git rebase integrates changes by replaying commits on top of the target branch, creating a linear project history. Unlike merge, it rewrites commit history. It should be used carefully, especially on shared branches.

q: In Git, what is the difference between 'git merge' and 'git rebase'?
options: Merge creates a new commit preserving history; rebase rewrites history creating a linear timeline
Rebase creates merge commits; merge creates a linear history
Merge only works locally; rebase works remotely
explanation: git merge preserves the complete history and creates a new merge commit, showing exactly when branches diverged and were rejoined. git rebase rewrites the project history by creating brand new commits, resulting in a cleaner, linear history.

q: What does 'git stash' command do?
options: Temporarily shelves changes so you can work on something else
Pushes changes to a remote repository
Creates a new branch with current changes
explanation: git stash temporarily shelves (or stashes) changes you've made to your working copy so you can work on something else and then come back and re-apply them later. Use 'git stash pop' to reapply the stashed changes.

q: Which Git command shows the commit history of the repository?
explanation: git log shows the commit history for the current branch. It displays commits in reverse chronological order showing commit hash, author, date, and commit message. Options like --oneline, --graph, --all provide different views.

q: In SVN (Subversion), what is the equivalent of Git's 'commit'?
explanation: In SVN, 'svn commit' sends your changes to the repository, similar to Git's commit and push combined. However, in Git, 'git commit' only saves changes locally, while 'git push' sends them to the remote repository.

q: Which of the following is a key difference between SVN and Git?
options: SVN is distributed; Git is centralized
Git is distributed; SVN is centralized
Both are distributed version control systems
Both are centralized version control systems
explanation: Git is a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS) where every developer has a full copy of the repository. SVN (Subversion) is a Centralized Version Control System (CVCS) where there is one central repository that all users commit to.

q: In SVN, what does 'svn update' do?
options: Sends local changes to the server
Fetches and merges latest changes from the repository to the working copy
explanation: 'svn update' brings changes from the repository into the working copy. It is equivalent to 'git pull' in Git. It merges the server's latest changes with your local working copy.

q: Which branching strategy in Git uses long-lived branches like 'main', 'develop', 'feature', 'release', and 'hotfix'?
explanation: Gitflow (by Vincent Driessen) uses long-lived branches: main (production), develop (integration), feature (new features), release (release preparation), and hotfix (urgent production fixes). It's suitable for projects with scheduled release cycles.

q: In change management, a Change Request (CR) typically goes through which sequence?
options: Approval â†’ Submission â†’ Analysis â†’ Implementation â†’ Verification
Submission â†’ Analysis â†’ Approval â†’ Implementation â†’ Verification
Analysis â†’ Submission â†’ Implementation â†’ Approval â†’ Verification
Submission â†’ Implementation â†’ Analysis â†’ Approval â†’ Verification
explanation: A Change Request (CR) typically follows: Submission â†’ Analysis (impact assessment) â†’ Approval (by CCB or change authority) â†’ Implementation â†’ Verification/Testing â†’ Closure. This ensures controlled and traceable changes.

q: CCB in configuration management stands for:
explanation: CCB stands for Change Control Board (also sometimes called Configuration Control Board). It is a group responsible for reviewing and approving or rejecting changes to configuration items, ensuring changes are managed in a controlled manner.

q: Release Management in software development primarily deals with:
options: Planning, scheduling, and controlling the build, test, and deployment of releases
explanation: Release Management is the process of managing, planning, scheduling, and controlling a software build through different stages and environments, including testing and deploying software releases.

q: In CI/CD, 'CI' stands for:
explanation: CI stands for Continuous Integration. It is the practice of frequently integrating code changes into a shared repository, where each integration is verified by automated builds and tests to detect problems early.

q: What is Continuous Delivery (CD) in a CI/CD pipeline?
options: Automatically deploying every code change to production
Ensuring code is always in a deployable state and can be released to production at any time with a manual trigger
Running automated tests on every commit
Automatically merging feature branches into main
explanation: Continuous Delivery ensures that code is always in a deployable state. It automates the release process up to production, but the final deployment to production requires a manual approval/trigger. It differs from Continuous Deployment where everything is automated including production deployment.

q: Which of the following is a popular CI/CD tool?
explanation: Jenkins is one of the most popular open-source CI/CD automation tools. It supports building, testing, and deploying software projects. Other CI/CD tools include GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, CircleCI, Travis CI, and Azure DevOps.

q: In a CI/CD pipeline, what is a 'pipeline stage'?
options: A logical group of steps/jobs executed sequentially or in parallel as part of the pipeline
explanation: A pipeline stage is a logical grouping of related tasks/jobs in a CI/CD pipeline. Common stages include: Source, Build, Test, Deploy to Staging, Integration Tests, Deploy to Production. Stages run sequentially, while jobs within a stage may run in parallel.

q: Git's 'git cherry-pick' command is used to:
options: Select the best code from multiple branches
Apply specific commits from one branch to another
Merge specific files from another branch
explanation: git cherry-pick applies the changes introduced by specific commits onto the current branch. It allows you to pick individual commits from one branch and apply them to another, without merging the entire branch.

q: What is a 'tag' in Git?
options: A reference that points to a specific commit, typically used to mark release versions
explanation: In Git, a tag is a reference that points to a specific commit in the repository's history. Tags are typically used to mark release points (v1.0, v2.0, etc.). There are two types: lightweight tags (just a pointer) and annotated tags (stored as full objects with metadata).

q: In ISO 9126, which quality sub-characteristic refers to the ease with which a software product can be diagnosed for deficiencies or causes of failures?
explanation: Analysability (under Maintainability in ISO 9126) refers to the ease with which the software product can be diagnosed for deficiencies, causes of failures, or parts to be modified. It measures how understandable the code is for debugging and analysis.

q: Which CMMI process area is associated with Level 2 and focuses on establishing policies and procedures for managing software projects?
explanation: Project Planning is a CMMI Level 2 process area. It involves establishing and maintaining plans that define project activities. Other Level 2 process areas include Requirements Management, Project Monitoring and Control, Supplier Agreement Management, Measurement and Analysis, and Process and Product Quality Assurance.

q: In Six Sigma, 'Black Belt' refers to:
options: A quality certification level for a Six Sigma professional who leads improvement projects
A stage in the DMAIC process
explanation: In Six Sigma, Black Belt is a certification/role level for a professional who leads complex improvement projects, has deep knowledge of Six Sigma methodology, and mentors Green Belts. The hierarchy is: White Belt â†’ Yellow Belt â†’ Green Belt â†’ Black Belt â†’ Master Black Belt.

q: What is 'git revert' used for?
options: Deletes a commit permanently from history
Creates a new commit that undoes the changes of a previous commit, preserving history
Resets the working directory to a clean state
explanation: git revert creates a new commit that undoes the changes introduced by a specified commit. Unlike git reset, it does not modify the existing commit history, making it safe to use on public/shared branches.

q: In SVN, what is a 'repository' compared to Git?
options: In SVN, each developer has a full repository copy; in Git only the server has the full copy
In SVN, the repository exists on a central server; in Git, each developer has a full repository clone
explanation: In SVN (centralized), the repository exists on a central server and developers have working copies (not full repos). In Git (distributed), each developer has a complete local copy of the entire repository history, enabling offline work and more flexible workflows.

q: Which concept in CI/CD refers to deploying the application automatically to production after every successful build and test?
explanation: Continuous Deployment automatically deploys every change that passes all automated tests directly to production without any manual intervention. It is the most advanced form of CI/CD. Continuous Delivery requires a manual approval step before production deployment.

q: What is a 'hotfix' branch in Gitflow?
options: A branch for long-term experimental features
A branch created to quickly fix a critical bug in the production release
A branch that replaces the main branch
explanation: In Gitflow, a hotfix branch is created directly from the main/master branch to quickly fix a critical bug in production. After the fix, it is merged back into both main (with a new version tag) and develop branches to ensure the fix is included in future releases.

q: Configuration Status Accounting in SCM refers to:
options: The financial cost of configuration management
Recording and reporting the status of configuration items and change requests
Verifying that configuration items match their specifications
explanation: Configuration Status Accounting is the SCM activity responsible for recording and reporting the information needed to manage a configuration effectively. It tracks the current status of configuration items, change requests, and the history of changes to the configuration.

q: Which ISO 25010 quality characteristic was newly introduced compared to ISO 9126?
explanation: Security is a new top-level quality characteristic in ISO 25010 that was not present as a main characteristic in ISO 9126 (it existed as a sub-characteristic of Functionality in ISO 9126). ISO 25010 also added Compatibility as a new main characteristic.

q: In Git, what does 'git fetch' do compared to 'git pull'?
options: git fetch merges remote changes; git pull only downloads them
git fetch downloads remote changes without merging; git pull downloads and merges
git fetch works only with SVN repositories
explanation: git fetch downloads changes from the remote repository to your local repository but does NOT merge them into your working branch. git pull is essentially 'git fetch' followed by 'git merge' â€” it downloads and immediately merges the remote changes into the current branch.

q: In a CI/CD pipeline, 'Infrastructure as Code' (IaC) tools like Terraform or Ansible are used for:
